title: Hansei Ichiran — A Visual Walkthrough
text:
Part one
  Background
  What Hansei Ichiran is, why the early Meiji government commissioned it, and a slow walk through how a single domain's entry is structured on the page.



  Part two
  The Dataset
  How 2,000-plus pages of brushed-script Japanese were turned into 286 rows × 107 columns. Per-variable provenance, source caveats, the 親藩 / 譜代 / 外様 framework.



  Part three
  The Paper's Argument
  Why the 1871 abolition of the feudal domains happened peacefully: the Political Coase Theorem applied. The retainer-burden evidence from Hansei Ichiran, the kazoku peerage as institutional side payment, and the 1884 rank assignments as ratification of a credible bargain.

title: Hansei Ichiran — A Visual Walkthrough
text:
Who this is for
A reader who knows nothing about Tokugawa Japan should be able to follow along. Every Japanese term is glossed at first appearance. Every claim has a citation on the page where it appears, and the full bibliography sits at the end.
A reader who knows the period well will find this useful as a quick reference to what is actually in the dataset, and where the source’s limits begin.

title: Hansei Ichiran — A Visual Walkthrough
text:
How to navigate

The left sidebar jumps between chapters.
Each chapter has its own right-hand table of contents for in-page sections.
Maps and images expand on click via the browser’s native zoom.
The bibliography aggregates every cited source in one place.

title: Hansei Ichiran — A Visual Walkthrough
text:
How this was built
The analytic dataset (analytic_wide.csv, 286 domains × 107 columns) is the result of:

OCR and manual verification of the 1870 Hansei Ichiran’s 2,000-plus pages (the original is held at the National Diet Library).
Cross-validation against published primary sources (Meiji Shiyō, Ōkuma papers, Tōkei Shūshi).
Hand-coded peerage rank assignments traced to 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治 (Kazoku tanjō: meiyo to taimen no Meiji, “Birth of the Peerage: Honor and Reputation in Meiji”)』(1994) and Kanpō 第307号 (1884).
Boshin alignment classifications from Esposito (2020), 工藤威 (2002), and Beasley (1972).

A complete per-column provenance reference (data_provenance.md) lists, for every column, the source, the citation guidance, the coverage rate, and any known caveats; it will be released alongside the dataset.

title: 2  What is Hansei Ichiran?
text:
2.1 Why 1870?
Hansei Ichiran literally means “han-system overview” — a survey of every daimyō (大名) domain (藩, han) compiled by the early Meiji government in 1870.
By 1870, the Meiji government had been in power for two years. The Tokugawa bakufu (幕府, the shōgunate) had been overthrown in the 1868–69 Boshin War (Boshin sensō), but the country was still administratively organized into roughly 280 semi-autonomous han, each ruled by a hereditary daimyō (feudal lord).
The new government needed to know, with precision, what each domain actually was: how big, how rich, how many soldiers, how many households, what taxes it collected. That knowledge was the prerequisite for the next step — abolishing the domains entirely, which happened the following year (1871, haihan chiken (廃藩置県)).
Hansei Ichiran is the document that captured the han system at the very moment before it was dismantled.

title: 2  What is Hansei Ichiran?
text:
TipWhy this matters for the paper



The paper applies the Political Coase Theorem to explain why Japan’s 1871 abolition of the feudal domains (haihan chiken) occurred peacefully rather than triggering a second civil war. The argument: facing mutual fiscal crises, the Meiji government and the daimyō reached a Coasean bargain — the state assumed domain debts and granted kazoku (華族, “peerage”) status, stipends, and government offices as institutionalised side payments, in exchange for sole ownership over taxation.
Hansei Ichiran is the quantitative basis for the paper’s claim that the daimyō themselves were facing structural insolvency by 1870. The headline statistic the paper draws from the survey is retainers per 1,000 koku — a measure of how heavily each domain’s hereditary stipend obligations weighed on its revenue base. The 1870 survey shows the burden had become unsustainable for most domains. That is what made the buyout attractive enough to accept without resistance. See the fiscal-distress chapter for the per-domain detail.

title: 2  What is Hansei Ichiran?
text:
2.2 What’s in the book
Hansei Ichiran is two volumes, roughly 1,000 pages each, totaling 2,000-plus printed pages of dense vertical-script Japanese. Within each volume, every domain gets an entry of one to four pages (depending on its size) in a standardised column format that makes the same data fields directly comparable across domains.
The two volumes cover complementary subjects:

Volume 1 — fiscal and demographic data. For each domain: the revenue assessment in its several variants (sōdaka (草高) / omotedaka (表高) / naidaka (内高) / genmai (現米), all explained later), tax receipts, total population and household counts, and the breakdown of households into samurai (侍) (shizoku (士族)), foot-rank retainers (sotsu (卒族)), commoners (heimin (平民)), and religious establishments.
Volume 2 — military and administrative data. For each domain: troop strength (infantry, cavalry, riflemen, artillery), the number of warships if any, and a roster of administrative officials (the appointed governor, senior councilors, junior councilors).

The two volumes are each sub-divided internally into several numbered sub-tables that group entries by domain size for printing convenience; that internal numbering has no analytic significance and the data fields are consistent within each volume. So when the walkthrough cites “Hansei Ichiran vol. 1” or “vol. 2”, that’s all the structural detail the reader needs.

title: 2  What is Hansei Ichiran?
section: 2.3 What the printed page looks like
text:
2.3 What the printed page looks like
Each domain’s entry is laid out in vertical columns reading right-to-left, in the brushed-style typography of the Meiji era. The first column is the domain’s name preceded by ○ (a circle marker). Subsequent columns list field labels and their values in kanji numerals.
Here’s a sample crop of one domain’s vol. 1 page (the entry for Saga, which is also a focal case for the paper):






NoteThe Saga gap





Saga (佐嘉藩, modern 佐賀藩) has a Volume 1 fiscal entry but is completely absent from Volume 2’s military rosters — the standard tables of troop strength, officer rosters, and warships that every other major domain has. This is a gap in the source itself, not an extraction failure, and the dataset substitutes 藤野保 (2010) as a published-source supplement for Saga’s military data.




Glossary of essential terms (used throughout the walkthrough)

藩 (han) — a feudal domain. By 1870, the political unit ruled by a daimyō.
大名 (daimyō) — the hereditary lord of a han. There were about 280 in 1870.
石高 (kokudaka) — the assessed rice-yield of a domain. 1 koku ≈ 150 kg of rice, the amount one adult would eat in a year. Used as a proxy for revenue, military obligation, and rank.
公侯伯子男 (kō-kō-haku-shi-dan) — the five tiers of the 1884 kazoku (華族) peerage: prince / marquess / count / viscount / baron.
草高 (sōdaka) / 表高 (omotedaka) / 現米 (genmai) — three different ways to measure a domain’s revenue. The Hansei Ichiran reports several of them. They are NOT interchangeable; see The Variables for why.
戊辰戦争 (Boshin sensō, “Boshin War”) — the 1868–69 civil war between Tokugawa loyalists and the Imperial coalition.
奥羽越列藩同盟 (Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei) — the Northern Alliance, the pro-Tokugawa coalition of ~31 northern domains during the Boshin War.

title: 2  What is Hansei Ichiran?
text:
2.4 How the original was accessed
The 1870 Hansei Ichiran is held in scan form at the National Diet Library (NDL), Japan’s national library. The project worked from:

The NDL scans, page-by-page
Carefully cropped sub-sections for each variable
Manual transcription cross-checked against OCR

The data extraction was an iterative process; the result is summarized in The Dataset.

Source: National Diet Library Digital Collections (https://dl.ndl.go.jp/). A documented extraction pipeline accompanies the dataset release.

title: 3  A walkthrough of one domain: Saga (佐嘉藩 / Hizen)
text:
3.1 What you’ll see on the page
To make Hansei Ichiran concrete, we’ll walk through the entry for Saga (佐嘉藩) — one of the four domains that led the 1868 Meiji Restoration. Saga is one of the paper’s focal cases.
A Saga entry in Hansei Ichiran reads from right to left in vertical columns. The first column always begins with the domain-name marker ○佐嘉藩 (note the old kanji 嘉 rather than the modern 賀). Hansei Ichiran’s Volume 1 and Table-6 fascicles use this pre-1870 form — but, as we’ll see below, its Volume 2 military fascicle (Table 9) switches to the modern 佐賀, which is exactly how Saga’s army entry eluded earlier old-kanji-only searches.

WarningOld kanji vs modern kanji



The 1870 print uses pre-Meiji-era character forms (旧字体, kyūjitai). The dataset uses the modern forms (新字体, shinjitai):

佐嘉藩 → 佐賀藩 (Saga)
鹿兒島藩 → 鹿児島藩 (Kagoshima / Satsuma)
金澤藩 → 金沢藩 (Kanazawa / Kaga)

An alias table bridges these.

TipWhy three different “kokudaka”?



Hansei Ichiran reports several different revenue measures because they meant different things in different bureaucratic contexts:

sōdaka / omotedaka (表高) = the nominal yield assessment used for protocol and rank
新田高 = newly reclaimed productive land added on top
gōdaka (合高) = the sum (what Saga calls 貮口合)
genmai (現米) = the actual rice flow currently collected, which is almost always much lower than sōdaka due to natural variation, land degradation, and tax-exemptions

This distinction matters for the paper: the 1884 peerage rank was determined by genmai, not sōdaka (per Asami 1994). See The 19 Deviations.

title: 3  A walkthrough of one domain: Saga (佐嘉藩 / Hizen)
section: 3.3 What Saga’s military block looks like (Volume 2, Table 9)
text:
3.3 What Saga’s military block looks like (Volume 2, Table 9)
For a long time this walkthrough reported that Saga’s Volume 2 military rosters were absent — the entry seemed to stop at the fiscal block, and the dataset carried a SOURCE_GAP marker. That was wrong, and it’s a good illustration of how the old-kanji conventions can hide an entry in plain sight.
A native-resolution re-read (2026-05-30) found that Saga’s military does appear — in Volume 2, Table 9 (第九), on book pages 429–430. The reason it had been missed: the Table-9 fascicle indexes and prints the domain as 佐賀藩 (the modern 賀), not the 佐嘉藩 (old 嘉) used in the Table-6 entry, so an old-kanji-only search slid right past it. Read at the page, the entry is unambiguous — the ○佐賀藩 marker is bracketed by 足利藩 (before it, whose own troops are marked “unrecorded”) and 佐倉藩 (after it, with its own 636-man army), so the block in between is Saga’s:



Field on page
Value




Formation
六大隊 (6 battalions)


士官 (officers)
864


砲士 (artillery soldiers)
180


兵士 (infantry)
1,440


砲卒 (artillery privates)
108


兵卒 (infantry privates)
260


樂卒 (bandsmen)
120


Total personnel
2,972


Cannon
36 門 (砲三十六門)



The scale — six battalions and 36 field guns — is exactly the modernised Nabeshima army you’d expect from the domain that built Japan’s first reverberatory furnace and manufactured Armstrong guns at Mietsu (三重津). So Hansei Ichiran is now a directly citable primary source for Saga’s 1870 standing army — the figure no longer has to be supplied from a secondary.
What Table 9 still does not record for Saga is warships: there is no ship line in the entry. For Saga’s navy — the Western-style 凌風丸 and 日進丸 acquired in the bakumatsu period — a published source is still needed (藤野保『佐賀藩』(2010), which also details the 大組 (daikumi) infantry organisation and the Armstrong-gun programme). So the division of labour is now clean: Hansei Ichiran gives the army (2,972 men, 36 guns); the secondary literature fills in the navy the survey omitted.

section: 3.4 What this looks like in the dataset
text:
3.4 What this looks like in the dataset






NoteWhat “1884 rank” means in the table below



The 1884 peerage rank is not assigned to a domain — it’s assigned to the head of the former-daimyō family that used to rule that domain. Domains had been abolished in 1871, thirteen years before the Peerage Ordinance.
When the table says “Saga: 1884 rank = marquess,” the literal claim is: Nabeshima Naohiro (the head of the Nabeshima family that had ruled Saga domain in 1870) received marquess rank from the Imperial Court in 1884. The walkthrough indexes the rank by the 1870 domain purely for visualisation.
Chapter 05 explains what the kazoku (華族) peerage system was, how the five ranks were assigned, and why it functioned as a credible-commitment mechanism in the paper’s argument.

title: 4  Walkthrough: Satsuma (薩摩藩 / 鹿児島藩)
text:
4.1 At a glance in the dataset
Satsuma is the southernmost focal-4 domain and arguably the most consequential to the Restoration outcome. Its army did the heavy lifting at Toba-Fushimi, and its leaders (Saigō Takamori, Ōkubo Toshimichi) ran the new Meiji government.

title: 4  Walkthrough: Satsuma (薩摩藩 / 鹿児島藩)
section: 4.2 Military capacity — what Hansei Ichiran records + what supplements say
text:
4.2 Military capacity — what Hansei Ichiran records + what supplements say
Hansei Ichiran reports 1,568 active troops on the 1870 roster. But Satsuma’s actual military capacity was far larger:

~8,000 troops deployed during the Boshin War (Boshin sensō) (per Esposito 2020)
Warships: Kasuga (春日), Hōō (鳳凰), Heiun (平運)
Modern small arms: Smith & Wesson, Armstrong, Minié rifles, plus 1 Gatling gun (one of three known to have been in Japan during Boshin)
samurai pool: ~244,700 shizoku + ~95,569 sotsu = ~340,000 samurai-class persons. The single largest samurai population of any domain.







TipWhy “1,568 active” vs “8,000 deployed”



Hansei Ichiran’s 兵隊 (heitai, troops) line records the standing modernised force on the books at the moment of survey. The deployable mobilisation could be three to five times higher because Satsuma’s traditional samurai class could be activated en masse for war. The 8,000 Boshin figure (per Esposito) reflects this mobilisation. The standing force is cited from Hansei Ichiran; the mobilised force from Esposito.
crumbs: 4  Walkthrough: Satsuma (薩摩藩 / 鹿児島藩)

title: 4  Walkthrough: Satsuma (薩摩藩 / 鹿児島藩)
section: 4.3 Why Satsuma got upgraded to prince
text:
4.3 Why Satsuma got upgraded to prince
Per the Asami (1994) genmai-threshold rule, Satsuma’s genmai = 298,709 koku puts it well into marquess range. The actual award was prince — a 2-tier upgrade.
The 1884 叙爵内規 explicitly reserved the prince rank for specific families:

The Tokugawa main line (徳川宗家)
The five Sekke kuge (公家) court houses (近衛, 鷹司, 九条, 一条, 二条)
Shimazu (Satsuma) and Mōri (Chōshū) — for Restoration role
Plus a few late additions (Itō 1907, etc.)

Shimazu’s prince rank is therefore an explicit Restoration reward written into the rules — not a discretionary case. Both the main line (Tadayoshi) and the cadet 玉里島津 line (Hisamitsu) received prince.

title: 5  Walkthrough: Chōshū (長州藩 / 山口藩 / 萩藩)
text:
5.1 At a glance in the dataset
Chōshū is the second pillar of the Sat-Chō Restoration coalition. Its Kiheitai (奇兵隊, “extraordinary corps”) under Takasugi Shinsaku pioneered mixed-class peasant-samurai (侍) units that defeated the bakufu (幕府, the shōgunate)’s Second Chōshū Expedition in 1866 — the military demonstration that made the Restoration possible.

title: 5  Walkthrough: Chōshū (長州藩 / 山口藩 / 萩藩)
text:
5.2 Military capacity — modernization first
Where Satsuma was big on traditional samurai, Chōshū was big on modernized doctrine. By the time of Boshin:

17 standing 大隊 (battalions) × 433 troops = 7,361 men in the active modernized force
Kiheitai (奇兵隊): originally 300 troops, expanded to 622 by Boshin — Japan’s first cross-class regular army unit, with samurai, peasants, and townsmen serving alongside each other
諸隊 (other militia corps): ~5,000 men at peak, consolidated to ~2,250 standing army post-Boshin
Warships: 4 named vessels (per Hansei Ichiran)
Modernized small arms: French Chassepots + Enfields + Minié rifles

The Chōshū army (and the doctrine it pioneered) became the template for the Imperial Japanese Army that Yamagata Aritomo would build after 1873.
crumbs: 5  Walkthrough: Chōshū (長州藩 / 山口藩 / 萩藩)

title: 5  Walkthrough: Chōshū (長州藩 / 山口藩 / 萩藩)
section: 5.3 Why Chōshū got upgraded to prince
text:
5.3 Why Chōshū got upgraded to prince
Same rule provision as Satsuma — the 1884 叙爵内規 explicitly named Mōri (Chōshū) for prince rank as a Restoration reward.
Mōri Motonori (毛利元徳), the last Chōshū daimyō (大名), received prince in 1884. The promotion to prince in 1884 was confirmed in the very first publication of the 1884 ennoblement decree (官報 第307号, 1884-07-08).

Boshin alignment: Esposito (2020) + Beasley (1972) + Boshin War (Boshin sensō) Wikipedia.

text:
6.1 At a glance in the dataset
Tosa is the third pillar of the Sat-Chō-Do-Hi coalition. The Yamauchi family was a comparatively late joiner to the Restoration cause — it was Tosa’s Tani Tateki and Itagaki Taisuke who organized Tosa’s Jinshōtai (迅衝隊) for the 1868 imperial campaign, and Sakamoto Ryōma’s Tosa-Ronin influence shaped the political settlement.

title: 6  Walkthrough: Tosa (土佐藩 / 高知藩)
text:
6.2 Military capacity
Tosa’s military structure is notable for a few reasons:

9,000 海陸兵員 (army + navy combined) per Hansei Ichiran 1870 vol. 2 — though with attribution caveats (the figure appears under “海陸兵員” in a position that may include Yamauchi’s own retainers + the contributing Jinshōtai)
Jinshōtai (迅衝隊): ~600 men, Itagaki Taisuke’s elite unit deployed during the 1868 advance
Spencer rifles — among the first repeating rifles in Japan, smuggled by Tosa via Sakamoto Ryōma’s Kameyama Shachū trading firm
郷士-inclusive samurai class: Tosa’s gōshi (rural samurai, lower in rank) were always counted as shizoku in Hansei Ichiran’s reporting — a wider definition than other domains used

title: 6  Walkthrough: Tosa (土佐藩 / 高知藩)
section: 6.3 Why Tosa stayed at marquess (no upgrade)
text:
6.3 Why Tosa stayed at marquess (no upgrade)
Tosa’s kokudaka of 495,486 + genmai consistent with marquess rank → rank-consistent with the threshold rule, no deviation.
This is interesting: despite Tosa being one of the four leading Restoration coalitions, the Yamauchi family did not receive a prince upgrade like Satsuma and Chōshū did. The prince rank was explicitly reserved for the two largest Restoration-leading houses by the 叙爵内規; Tosa and Saga, while also leading the Restoration, were not named in that exception clause.
This is one of the cleaner empirical asymmetries the paper documents: all four Restoration-leading domains were anti-bakufu, but only the two largest got prince upgrades. Saga and Tosa stayed at the kokudaka-mechanical marquess level.

title: 7  Walkthrough: Kaga (加賀藩 / 金沢藩)
section: 7.2 Military capacity — paradoxically the most complete data
text:
7.2 Military capacity — paradoxically the most complete data
Kaga is one of the only 8 COMPLETE-RICH military entries in the entire dataset — its Hansei Ichiran vol. 2 record includes troops, officers, warships, and artillery in full detail. This is partly because:

Kaga had no real Boshin combat, so its 1870 establishment was unchanged
Kaga’s bureaucracy was unusually thorough about reporting
The Maeda family’s ~1.35 million koku assessment funded a substantial paper army

But despite this paper army, Kaga did not deploy in the Boshin War (Boshin sensō).
crumbs: 7  Walkthrough: Kaga (加賀藩 / 金沢藩)

title: 7  Walkthrough: Kaga (加賀藩 / 金沢藩)
section: 7.3 Why Kaga’s “anti-bakufu” classification is a late-mover label
text:
7.3 Why Kaga’s “anti-bakufu” classification is a late-mover label
Kaga’s recorded Boshin alignment = “anti-bakufu / pro-restoration” — but this reflects end-of-war posture, not active participation.
The narrative:

Early 1868: Kaga initially favors the shōgun (将軍) (Maeda’s traditional bakufu-loyalty)
Late January 1868: Toba-Fushimi battle ends; Tokugawa Yoshinobu surrenders
February 1868: Maeda publicly submits to the new Imperial Hokuriku pacification army
Throughout the Boshin War (戊辰戦争): Kaga sends no significant force to either side

So Kaga is “anti-bakufu” only in the sense that it ratified the imperial directive once that was clearly the winning side. Beasley (1972) is silent on Kaga as an active combatant; the JP Wikipedia article describes Kaga’s stance as cautious late-acceptance.






TipWhy this matters for the empirical analysis



Kaga’s case shows why the simple Boshin-alignment label is a noisy treatment variable in isolation. A binary “anti-bakufu vs pro-bakufu” doesn’t distinguish active Restoration leaders (Satsuma, Chōshū) from late-mover non-combatants (Kaga, plus 200+ smaller domains). The paper accounts for this with auxiliary citations and by focusing analyses on the genmai-deviation subsample where the action actually lives.

kokudaka 1.35 million → marquess range mechanically (Maeda was the largest tozama, but still tozama). The 1884 ennoblement followed the rule exactly: Maeda Toshitsugu received marquess. No deviation; no Restoration reward.

8.1.2 The historiographical debate
Lebra (1993) frames Aizu’s viscount rank as a Boshin punishment — Aizu was a major bakufu loyalist (Matsudaira Katamori was Kyoto Protector and a key Tokugawa loyalist), defeated at the Aizu siege (October-November 1868), and got the lowest peerage rank that any major domain received.
Asami (1994) reframes: the punishment was the territorial reduction from 230k to 30k koku, not the rank. Once the territory was reduced, the genmai-rule mechanically yielded viscount. The rank itself is rule-consistent.
Either framing yields the same outcome. The paper records both interpretations.


8.1.3 Military capacity
The Hansei Ichiran record for 斗南藩 reflects the post-reduction reality. For Aizu’s pre-Boshin military:

Four-corps age-graded system (Suzakutai 18-35, Seiryūtai 35-49, Byakkotai 16-17, Genbutai 50+)
Byakkotai (White Tigers): the famous teenage corps; ~300 youth, many of whom committed seppuku at the fall of Wakamatsu Castle
Modernization: limited compared to Chōshū/Satsuma; mostly traditional armor + percussion muskets

(Source: Esposito 2020.)

8.2.2 Why Sendai matters as a paper case
Sendai is the canonical example of the Lebra-vs-Asami debate:

Lebra (1993): Sendai was a Tokugawa ally → Boshin defeat → downgrade from marquess to count = explicit Boshin punishment
Asami (1994): Sendai’s 1869 genmai was 67,740 koku → squarely in count range mechanically → no punishment, just the rule

Both interpretations yield the same observed outcome (count). The dataset records both deviation values (Lebra framing alongside Asami framing) so the paper can present whichever baseline is appropriate.






NoteA third framing — the Kanpō #307 「依父勳功特授伯爵」 finding (added 2026-05-25)





The project’s 2026-05-25 transcription of Kanpō 第307号 (the 1884-07-08 government gazette publishing the ennoblement decree) records a third formal categorization for Sendai-Date:

從五位　伊達 宗基　← 依父勳功特授伯爵 (Junior Fifth Rank, Date Munemoto, Count rank specially conferred by the FATHER’S meritorious service)

The 「依父勳功特授」 prefix puts Date Munemoto on the merit-based ennoblement track — the same legal track as the 83 Restoration architects (Itō Hirobumi, Yamagata Aritomo, Inoue Kaoru, etc.). The Sendai count rank is therefore not lineage-mechanical in the way most count-tier ranks were; it’s an inherited merit ennoblement on his father’s recorded service.
The three frames are not mutually exclusive — they describe different layers of the same outcome:








Frame
Source
What it says




Boshin-penalty
Lebra (1993)
The territorial reduction from ~620k → ~280k post-Boshin was the punishment, lowering the kokudaka basis


Genmai-mechanical
Asami (1994)
The post-reduction genmai (67,740 koku) put Sendai in count band; the rank was the threshold-rule outcome


Patrilineal-merit
Kanpō #307 (2026-05-25)
The formal legal track was 「依父勳功特授伯爵」 — merit-track ennoblement inherited from father’s service, distinct from both pure-lineage and pure-merit



For paper writing: Sendai’s Count rank was both the genmai-threshold mechanical outcome (Asami) AND a formal patrilineal merit-track ennoblement (Kanpō #307), atop a post-Boshin territorial reduction (Lebra). See kazoku_rei_decree_kanpo_307.md for the verbatim Kanpō entry and the broader 83-person merit-track roster.
3 years old at haihan-chiken (1871), 16 at the kazoku-rei (1884) — Date Munemoto was too young for his own meritorious service to ground a 1884 ennoblement; the patrilineal-merit framing was the legal instrument that handled that age fact.





8.2.3 Military capacity — a data-quality correction
Sendai had a documented data-quality issue: the original extraction had the sotsu-class headcount of 18,913 mislabelled as the total-troops figure (the OCR conflated 兵卒 with 兵士). The post-fix state:

Total troops = 14,262 (兵士 員 — the actual active force, per Hansei Ichiran vol. 2)
sotsu-class headcount = 18,913 (sotsu 同人口 — sotsu-class demographics, not troops)

See the data-quality chapter for the audit trail behind this correction.


8.2.4 Northern Alliance leadership
Sendai was the lead signatory of the Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei (奥羽越列藩同盟, Northern Alliance) at the White Stone Conference of May 6, 1868. The alliance covenant was drafted there. Aizu and Sendai are the only two Northern Alliance members that Beasley (1972) names by name; the full 31-member roster is from 工藤威『奥羽列藩同盟の基礎的研究 (Ōu reppan dōmei no kisoteki kenkyū, “Basic Research on the Northern Alliance”)』(2002).

Primary: Hansei Ichiran (1870), NDL Digital Collections. Population: Meiji Shiyō (Shūshikyoku). Military: Hansei Ichiran vol. 2; Esposito (2020) Northern Alliance overview. kazoku rank: 浅見雅男 (1994); Kanpō 第307号 (1884-07-08). Boshin alignment: 工藤威『奥羽列藩同盟の基礎的研究』(2002); Esposito (2020); Beasley (1972) p. 298 (the only English-language source that names Sendai + Aizu as Northern Alliance leaders). Domain category correction: 児玉幸多 ed.『藩史総覧 (Hanshi sōran, “Comprehensive Survey of Han History”)』(1977) confirms 仙台藩 is canonical tozama (Date family was second-largest tozama after Maeda).

title: 9  Walkthrough: Northern Alliance defectors (Kubota, Hirosaki, Matsumae)
text:
9.1 久保田藩 (Kubota / 秋田)
The Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei (奥羽越列藩同盟, the “Northern Alliance”) was the pro-Tokugawa military coalition formed in May 1868 by ~31 northern domains. But the coalition wasn’t monolithic: at least 9 signatory domains defected to the Imperial side during the war, and the trajectories of those defectors are paper-relevant.
This chapter walks through three of the most consequential defectors:

9.1.2 The defection narrative
Kubota signed the Northern Alliance covenant at the Second White Stone Conference (May 11, 1868). But the Satake family had a longstanding rivalry with neighboring Nanbu (Morioka, also a Northern Alliance member) — and a strong Tokugawa-loyalty deficit going back to Sekigahara, when the Satake were transferred from their original Mito domain to Akita as a demotion for siding with the western coalition.
By July 1868, Kubota had defected to the Imperial side. The Satake’s resentment toward the bakufu (200+ years old) probably mattered more than ideology.
The Satake family received marquess rank in 1884 — consistent with their kokudaka of 333k. No “Restoration reward,” no penalty — just the mechanical rule applied to a tozama house above 150k koku.

9.2.2 The defection narrative
Hirosaki is at the very northern tip of Honshū — historically the Tsugaru family’s domain, the archetypal far-northern tozama. The Tsugaru had been semi-independent vassals of the Nanbu (Morioka) before being recognized as independent daimyō at the 1590 Odawara campaign, and there was lingering bad blood between Tsugaru and Nanbu.
Hirosaki signed the Northern Alliance covenant at the Second White Stone Conference but defected shortly thereafter, partly motivated by Tsugaru/Nanbu rivalry (Nanbu was a major Northern Alliance combatant; if Nanbu wins, Tsugaru loses). Hirosaki’s troops fought against the imperial forces briefly before switching, then participated in the imperial advance.
The Tsugaru family received count in 1884. Their genmai (現米) of 142k put them just below the marquess threshold; the actual rank is rule-consistent. No Restoration upgrade despite the defection — defecting to the winning side wasn’t enough on its own to merit a kazoku-rank upgrade beyond the mechanical level.

9.3.2 The defection narrative
Matsumae was Japan’s only daimyō domain in Hokkaidō (then called Ezo). The Matsumae family held a small, geographically isolated, fishery-and-trade-based domain that mattered less for its rice yield (30k koku is small) than for its position controlling Honshu’s gateway to Hokkaido.
Matsumae signed the Northern Alliance covenant at the Second White Stone Conference but defected via internal coup on July 28, 1868: a faction within the Matsumae retainers seized control and committed the domain to the Imperial side. This dramatic switch had downstream consequences:

It deprived the Ezo Republic (Enomoto Takeaki’s October 1868–June 1869 holdout) of a friendly base on Hokkaido
The defection was so abrupt that the family’s bookkeeping records were ambiguous about which side they were on for several weeks

The Matsumae family received viscount in 1884 — consistent with their small genmai of 23k koku.

title: 9  Walkthrough: Northern Alliance defectors (Kubota, Hirosaki, Matsumae)
section: 9.4 What the three teach us about Boshin alignment
text:
9.4 What the three teach us about Boshin alignment
These three cases illustrate why the project codes Boshin alignment as an end-of-war position rather than the initial commitment:

All three signed the Northern Alliance covenant in mid-May 1868 (pro-bakufu commitment)
All three defected to the Imperial side by mid-to-late summer 1868
The 1884 ennoblement treated them as anti-bakufu / pro-restoration — but did not reward them with upgrades: their ranks are rule-consistent with their genmai

This is in tension with the simpler narrative that “Boshin loyalty matters.” Defecting to the winning side was good enough to avoid the territorial reduction that fell on the non-defecting Northern Alliance members (Aizu, Nanbu, Nihonmatsu, etc.) — but it was not enough to merit explicit rank upgrades. The 9 documented Restoration-service upgrades all went to active anti-bakufu leaders, not late-defectors.

Citation chain: Northern Alliance membership + defection events: 工藤威『奥羽列藩同盟の基礎的研究 (Ōu reppan dōmei no kisoteki kenkyū, “Basic Research on the Northern Alliance”)』(岩田書院 2002), transcribed in the JP Wikipedia 奥羽越列藩同盟 article. The Wikipedia article reproduces the Kudō roster with footnote-level page references back to the monograph. kazoku ranks: 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治 (Kazoku tanjō: meiyo to taimen no Meiji, “Birth of the Peerage: Honor and Reputation in Meiji”)』(1994); Kanpō 第307号 (1884-07-08). Family histories: Standard scholarly references for each family — 角川日本姓氏歴史人物大辞典 entries for 佐竹家, 津軽家, 松前家; 児玉幸多 ed.『藩史総覧 (Hanshi sōran, “Comprehensive Survey of Han History”)』(1977). Matsumae internal coup: per JP Wikipedia 松前藩 article (sourced to 函館市史 and 北海道史 archives).

The Tokugawa sankyō (徳川三卿) — Hitotsubashi (一橋), Tayasu (田安), and Shimizu (清水) — are an unusual case: three high-status hatamoto-rank lines of the shogunal family that had no domain but still received kazoku (華族) rank in 1884. The project includes them for completeness, and they show up as a row in analytic_wide.csv despite having no kokudaka (石高), no troops, no population.

title: 10  Walkthrough: Tokugawa sankyō (御三卿) (一橋 + 田安 + 清水)
text:
10.2 Why count, not viscount?
The sankyō received count rank in 1884 despite having no kokudaka — the 叙爵内規 (assignment rules) had an explicit exception clause for the sankyō houses, ranking them at count level as a courtesy reflecting their shogunal-family status. From the 1884 kazoku Law internal rules:

「徳川氏ノ親族中、三卿（一橋・田安・清水）ノ各家ハ伯爵ニ叙ス」 Among the Tokugawa family, each of the sankyō houses (一橋・田安・清水) shall be ranked at count.

This is one of the several explicit exception clauses written into the 1884 rules:








Exception
Houses affected
Outcome




Tokugawa main → prince
Iesato (Shizuoka)
Tokugawa-main exception


Gosanke → marquess (regardless of genmai (現米))
Owari, Kii, Mito
Gosanke clause


Sankyō → count
Hitotsubashi, Tayasu, Shimizu
Sankyō clause


Shimazu (Satsuma) + Mōri (Chōshū) → prince
Restoration leaders
Restoration reward



So if you look at the genmai-deviation table in chapter 07, the 3 pro-bakufu (幕府) rank-upgrades (Shizuoka prince, Mito marquess) are explained by these clauses — not by individual discretion.

title: 10  Walkthrough: Tokugawa sankyō (御三卿) (一橋 + 田安 + 清水)
section: 10.3 The empirical implication for the paper
text:
10.3 The empirical implication for the paper
The sankyō cases are useful null controls in the paper’s regression of 1884 kazoku rank on Boshin alignment + kokudaka:

Their rank is rule-bound (count, by sankyō clause), not Boshin-determined
Their alignment is technically pro-Tokugawa-family but they had no military role in the Boshin War (Boshin sensō)
Including them in the regression sample shows the kokudaka-mechanical rule has limits — for these 2 cases, kokudaka = 0 and rank = count, breaking the rule

Practical handling: the paper analyzes a subset that excludes the sankyō (since the mechanical rule doesn’t apply to them), but reports them as documented exceptions.

title: 10  Walkthrough: Tokugawa sankyō (御三卿) (一橋 + 田安 + 清水)
text:
10.4 Tokugawa Yoshinobu — the post-shōgun
Beyond the 1884 ennoblement, an interesting later story: Tokugawa Yoshinobu himself (the 15th shōgun, who had been Hitotsubashi household head before becoming shōgun) received prince rank in 1902 — 34 years after the Restoration, 14 years after Mito’s marquess→prince upgrade. This was a personal honour to the former shōgun, not a family-line ennoblement, and it is not in the 286-domain dataset because Yoshinobu’s personal line was treated separately from both the Tokugawa main line and Hitotsubashi.

Citation chain: Sankyō clause: 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治 (Kazoku tanjō: meiyo to taimen no Meiji, “Birth of the Peerage: Honor and Reputation in Meiji”)』(1994), pp. 109–110. 1884 kazoku-rei text: Kanpō 第307号 (1884-07-08), NDL pid 2943511. Family histories: 児玉幸多 ed.『藩史総覧 (Hanshi sōran, “Comprehensive Survey of Han History”)』(1977); JP Wikipedia sankyō article with footnote chain. Yoshinobu 1902 ennoblement: 官報 第5683号 (1902-06-04), NDL Digital Collections.

title: 11  Walkthrough: The 5 small-domain Restoration-service upgrades
text:
11.1 The pattern
This chapter is structured differently from the previous walkthroughs. Instead of going domain-by-domain through one focal case, it walks through a pattern — the 5 viscount → count upgrades that constitute the cleanest empirical evidence for the paper’s central claim.
Per the Asami (1994) genmai (現米) rule, all 5 of these domains should have received viscount rank (genmai well below 50,000 koku). All 5 actually received count — a one-tier upgrade. And all 5 were on the anti-bakufu (幕府) / pro-restoration side.

ImportantWhy this pattern matters for the paper



The 19 genmai-rule deviations split into four categories: - 3 prince upgrades (Satsuma, Chōshū, Shizuoka) — Restoration prize + Tokugawa exception - 3 marquess upgrades (Mito, Fukui, Uwajima) — gosanke clause + Restoration role - 5 viscount→count upgrades (this chapter) — pure Restoration service - 8 viscount→baron downgrades — former tsukegarō (付家老) and hatamoto (旗本) houses, elevated to daimyō status only at the Restoration; orthogonal to Boshin
The viscount → count upgrades are the cleanest evidence because:

They don’t have a separate rule exception (no gosanke clause, no Tokugawa-main exception)
They’re small enough that the rank could plausibly have gone either way
Their kokudaka is below 50k, so the rule strictly says viscount
But they got count instead — and all 5 are on the anti-bakufu side

This is the empirical core of “Boshin / Restoration alignment matters for rank deviations, conditional on revenue.”

section: 11.7 What this teaches about the paper’s empirical claim
text:
11.7 What this teaches about the paper’s empirical claim
The cleanest version of the paper’s central claim:

Of the 9 daimyō (大名) families that received explicit kazoku (華族)-rank upgrades for Restoration-era service (excluding the structural exceptions like the gosanke clause), 9 of 9 were anti-bakufu / pro-Restoration aligned. The 5 viscount → count upgrades are the largest subgroup of this pattern.

The 4 marquess+prince upgrades (Satsuma, Chōshū, Fukui, Uwajima) are well-known. The 5 viscount → count upgrades are the less-famous, more-numerous, statistically more-persuasive part of the same pattern — and the paper highlights all 9 together as a clean empirical core.

Citation chain: Rank assignments: 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治 (Kazoku tanjō: meiyo to taimen no Meiji, “Birth of the Peerage: Honor and Reputation in Meiji”)』(リブロポート 1994); Kanpō 第307号 (1884-07-08), NDL pid 2943511. Individual family histories: 児玉幸多 ed.『藩史総覧 (Hanshi sōran, “Comprehensive Survey of Han History”)』(1977); Kokushi daijiten entries on 大村家, 島津家, 宗家, 亀井家, 大給松平家. Restoration roles: Esposito (2020) Osprey Men-at-Arms 530; Jansen (1961/1994); Beasley (1972) ch. XI. Ōgyū Yutaka biography: 国史大辞典 entry on 大給恒; the 賞勲局 founding history. 津和野/Kamei Koremi: 国史大辞典 entry on 亀井茲監; JP Wikipedia 津和野藩 article with footnote chain.

title: 12  Walkthrough: The 29 shinpan (Tokugawa cadet) houses
text:
12.1 The 29, in distribution
The shinpan (親藩) category (29 domains, ~10% of the dataset) is the smallest of the three major Tokugawa-era classifications but the most internally structured. Almost every shinpan traces back, through fewer than ten generations, to either Tokugawa Ieyasu himself or to a small set of Matsudaira branches related to him. This chapter walks the cluster by lineage: the three big shogunal-family houses (gosanke (御三家)), the Tokugawa main line after 1868 (Shizuoka), and the major Matsudaira sub-clades — Echizen-Matsudaira, Hisamatsu-Matsudaira, and Mito-Matsudaira.
By 1884 kazoku (華族) rank (family rank, indexed to the domain that family ruled in 1870):
Note the floor: no shinpan house received baron. The 1884 rule effectively guaranteed shinpan a minimum of viscount, regardless of how small their genmai (現米) had become. The smallest shinpan in the dataset (谷地藩, kokudaka (石高) unrecorded; 母里藩 at 10,000 koku; 清崎藩 at 10,000 koku) still received viscount.
By Boshin alignment:
The 21 “anti-bakufu” shinpan are mostly end-state classifications: Tokugawa-cadet branches that complied with the post-Toba-Fushimi imperial directive without active resistance. The 6 actively-pro-bakufu shinpan are the cases where the lord stayed loyal to the bakufu through the Boshin War (Boshin sensō) — most prominently Aizu (covered in chapter 02f), Kuwana, Shizuoka (post-relocation), and a few of the Mito-Matsudaira branches.

text:
12.2 The gosanke — Owari, Kii, Mito
The three flagship Tokugawa-cadet houses, established in the early 17th century by three of Ieyasu’s sons. By 1884 all three received marquess rank, two through the standard rule (Owari, Kii) and one through the explicit gosanke clause (Mito).

12.2.1 尾張藩 (Owari) — Nagoya, the senior gosanke house







Family
尾張徳川家


1884 recipient
徳川義勝 (later 徳川義礼)

kokudaka (Hansei Ichiran sōdaka (草高))
853,115 koku


Genmai
327,468 koku


1884 outcome
marquess (侯爵)


Boshin role
Anti-bakufu — Owari defected to the imperial side under Tokugawa Yoshikatsu’s leadership



Owari was founded by Tokugawa Yoshinao (徳川義直), Ieyasu’s ninth son. It was the senior of the three gosanke and the largest with about 619,500 koku of true holding (plus shinden = 853k full kokudaka). The Owari-Tokugawa anchored bakufu legitimacy in central Japan and held the senior place in court protocol among the cadet branches.
The Boshin alignment is striking: a Tokugawa cadet of this seniority might have been expected to defend the bakufu. Instead Tokugawa Yoshikatsu (徳川義勝, then head of the house) led Owari into the imperial coalition in January 1868, and Owari forces participated in the northern campaigns against the Northern Alliance. This defection mattered for the imperial cause both militarily (Owari had ~1,500 troops + Western-style cannon) and symbolically (a sanke house repudiating the bakufu).
The marquess rank in 1884 was the standard genmai-rule outcome (327k genmai is comfortably in marquess range); the gosanke clause didn’t need to fire for Owari.


12.2.2 和歌山藩 (Wakayama / Kii) — the second gosanke house

Boshin role
Anti-bakufu (late-Restoration compliance)



Kii was founded by Tokugawa Yorinobu (徳川頼宣), Ieyasu’s tenth son. Kii is historically important as the cadet branch that produced the 8th shōgun (将軍) Yoshimune (1716–1745) when the main Tokugawa line failed — establishing that the gosanke could supply a shōgun.
By the bakumatsu period Kii’s main political importance was structural (as a shōgun-reserve), and the family followed the late-Restoration imperial-consolidation path. The Kii 付家老 houses (Mizuno of 新宮 and Andō of 田辺) were detached at the Restoration; both received baron rank in 1884 under the post-1868-elevation rule (see chapter 07 §D).
The 1884 marquess rank was the standard outcome (genmai 279k clears the marquess threshold).


12.2.3 水戸藩 (Mito) — the junior gosanke house, intellectually outsized







Family
水戸徳川家


1884 recipient

kokudaka (Hansei Ichiran gōdaka (合高))

1884 outcome
marquess (侯爵) (via gosanke clause)


Boshin role
Pro-bakufu by family-affiliation (Mito’s internal politics were violently divided)



Mito was founded by Tokugawa Yorifusa (徳川頼房), Ieyasu’s eleventh son. Smaller than Owari and Kii (about 280,000 koku formal kokudaka with 領内 increments), but disproportionately important politically and intellectually.
Mito is famous for mitogaku (水戸学) — the Confucian-historical school that, by the 19th century, was producing strident loyalist-imperialist ideology even though Mito was itself a Tokugawa-cadet branch. The contradiction (Tokugawa-blood family producing the intellectual foundations for the Restoration) destabilised Mito politics throughout the 1860s. The 天狗党の乱 (Tengutō Rebellion, 1864) was a Mito-internal civil war between the radical-loyalist 天狗党 faction and the bakufu-loyalist 諸生党 faction. Casualties were severe and the rebellion took years to settle.
The Mito branch also produced the 15th shōgun Yoshinobu (徳川慶喜), who was Mito-born before being adopted into Hitotsubashi and then becoming shōgun in 1866. Yoshinobu’s resignation in 1867 and the bakufu’s military defeat in 1868 closed the cycle.
In 1884 Mito’s genmai of 58,553 koku put it in count range — but the gosanke clause of the kazoku-rei elevated it to marquess. This is the unique case where the gosanke clause actually fired (Owari and Kii cleared the marquess threshold on the standard rule). The deviation is documented in chapter 07 §B.

After the bakufu’s military defeat in 1868, the Tokugawa main line was stripped of its Edo-region holdings (formerly some 4 million koku of tenryō plus the shogunal domains) and relocated to a much-reduced 700,000-koku Sunpu (modern Shizuoka) territory. The relocation made the main Tokugawa line, for the first time in 250 years, a daimyō (大名) in the conventional sense — they had been the suzerain of all daimyō, not one of them.
The 1884 prince rank applied an explicit “Tokugawa main-line clause” in the kazoku-rei, separate from both the sanke clause (which applied to Owari/Kii/Mito) and the Restoration-leader clause (which applied to Shimazu/Mōri). 徳川家達 (1863–1940), great-grandson of the 11th shōgun Ienari, was the recipient. This is the methodological centrepiece for the paper’s claim that the 1884 ennoblements ranked by what the rules said, not by who won the war: the defeated central party received the highest rank.
See chapter 07 §A for the full Shizuoka deviation block.

Universal anti-bakufu alignment in the data — the entire branch went over to the imperial side. The key actor was 松平春嶽 (松平慶永, 1828–1890), the lord of Fukui, who was a 四賢侯 figure and led the public-deliberation (公議政体派) faction. Fukui is covered in detail in chapter 07 §B as one of the three count→marquess upgrades.

TipKuwana reclassification



桑名藩 was originally coded fudai in some popular sources but is treated here as shinpan. The reasoning: Kuwana was held by the Hisamatsu-Matsudaira branch from 1710 until the Restoration; since the other two Hisamatsu-Matsudaira domains (伊予松山, 今治) are already coded shinpan, consistency requires Kuwana also be shinpan.
The Hisamatsu-Matsudaira are sometimes ambiguously classified in popular references because the founder Sadakatsu was Ieyasu’s half-brother (not a direct son), and because the lineage took a Matsudaira surname rather than the Tokugawa one. Academic sources (児玉幸多 ed.『藩史総覧 (Hanshi sōran, “Comprehensive Survey of Han History”)』1977; 中川 学『松平氏由緒書 (Matsudaira-shi yuishogaki, “Matsudaira Lineage Records”)』1989) consistently treat them as shinpan.
See the methodology note in chapter 03c for the full context.

title: 12  Walkthrough: The 29 shinpan (Tokugawa cadet) houses
text:
12.8 The Aizu cluster — cross-link only
The shinpan lineage most prominently associated with the Boshin War — the Aizu-Matsudaira (会津松平) — is covered in detail in chapter 02f: Aizu and Sendai. The bakumatsu-era Aizu lord 松平容保 was Kyoto Shoshidai under the bakufu’s final political reorganisation and led the pro-bakufu Northern Alliance after Toba-Fushimi. After Aizu’s defeat in 1868 the family was relocated to 斗南藩 (3 万石) in northern Honshu and received only viscount rank in 1884 — at the time framed as a Boshin punishment, but per chapter 07 understood now as a mechanical genmai-rule outcome (post-Boshin genmai of 7,380 koku is squarely in viscount range).

title: 12  Walkthrough: The 29 shinpan (Tokugawa cadet) houses
text:
12.9 What the shinpan cluster teaches
Three points:

The lineage structure is dense and traceable. Almost every shinpan traces back through documented branch points to either Ieyasu, the gosanke, or one of the major Matsudaira branches (Echizen, Hisamatsu, Mito, Fukamizō, Yoshii). The category is not just an administrative label — it tracks an actual genealogical structure.
The 1884 ennoblement effectively floored shinpan at viscount, ensuring no Tokugawa-cadet family fell below the daimyō-rank tier. Combined with the standard genmai rule, the result was a compressed rank distribution across shinpan: 1 prince, 4 marquess, 4 count, 20 viscount, 0 baron.
Boshin alignment within shinpan was lineage-segregated. The Echizen-Matsudaira branches were uniformly anti-bakufu (driven by Shungaku of Fukui); the Hisamatsu-Matsudaira branches divided (Iyo-Matsuyama + Kuwana pro-bakufu; Imabari anti-bakufu); the Mito-Matsudaira branches divided (most anti, Tsuruta pro, Shishido/Kiyozaki neutral); the Aizu-Matsudaira lineage went down with the bakufu. The category-level “shinpan = pro-bakufu” intuition is wrong in the Boshin data: 21 of 29 are coded anti-bakufu.


Citation chain for this chapter:
Genealogical structure (Matsudaira branches): 中川 学『松平氏由緒書 (Matsudaira-shi yuishogaki, “Matsudaira Lineage Records”)』(三河叢書 1989) — the standard reference for the Matsudaira sub-lineage tree. JP Wikipedia 御三家 / 越前松平家 / 久松松平家 / 水戸徳川家 articles, used as quick lookups for branch dates and disputed cases.
Per-domain narratives: 児玉幸多 ed.『藩史総覧』新人物往来社 (1977) — canonical han-classification compendium; Kokushi daijiten (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1979–1997) entries on each daimyō house.
Boshin alignments: Beasley, W.G. The Meiji Restoration. Stanford University Press (1972), ch. XI; 工藤威『奥羽列藩同盟の基礎的研究 (Ōu reppan dōmei no kisoteki kenkyū, “Basic Research on the Northern Alliance”)』(岩田書院 2002) for the Northern Alliance members; Esposito, Gabriele. Japanese Armies 1868–1877: The Boshin War and Satsuma Rebellion. Osprey Men-at-Arms 530 (2020) for the focal cases.
mitogaku and the Tengutō Rebellion: 吉田俊純『水戸学と明治維新 (Mitogaku to Meiji Ishin, “Mitogaku and the Meiji Restoration”)』吉川弘文館 (2003) — academic reference on Mito’s internal politics.
Aizu-Matsudaira and the Boshin punishment / mechanical-genmai reframing: 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治 (Kazoku tanjō: meiyo to taimen no Meiji, “Birth of the Peerage: Honor and Reputation in Meiji”)』(リブロポート 1994), and see chapter 07 of this walkthrough.

title: 13  The 286 domains
text:
13.1 Map: kokudaka (石高) across all 286 domains
The project’s dataset covers 286 daimyō (大名) domains — the full universe of han enumerated in Hansei Ichiran 1870, plus a handful of post-1869 administrative renames the source treats as separate entries.
The map below shows each domain colored by its kokudaka — the revenue assessment in koku of rice. This is the paper’s main predictor variable.

title: 13  The 286 domains
text:
Figure 13.1: Domain kokudaka, 1870 — the composite kokudaka value mapped on the daimyō-domain base map. Color uses a log scale because revenues range from ~10,000 koku (smallest viscount-tier domains) to ~1.4 million koku (Kaga, the largest). Grey polygons = a daimyō domain with no kokudaka value. Blank white = land that was not a daimyō domain (shogunal tenryō, hatamoto holdings, temple / court land).









NoteWhy so much of the map is blank white



The base map draws only the 206 daimyō-domain polygons in the 1664 Harvard CHGIS shapefile. Roughly a quarter of Edo-period Japan was not a daimyō domain — it was land the Tokugawa shogunate held directly (tenryō 天領), land held by the shogun’s bannermen (hatamoto 旗本), or temple / shrine / imperial-court land. None of that has a daimyō, so none of it is in a 286-domain dataset, and it shows as blank white. The large white expanse through central Honshū (around Kōfu and Hida) is mostly shogunal tenryō. See the territorial-changes chapter for the full explanation, including the white-vs-grey distinction.

title: 13  The 286 domains
text:
13.2 How to read the map
A few orientation points:

The four colored labels (Satsuma, Chōshū, Tosa, Saga) are the Sat-Chō-Do-Hi coalition that led the Restoration. Three are in Kyushu (Satsuma, Chōshū at the western tip of Honshu, Saga) and one is on Shikoku (Tosa).
Aizu, Sendai, Yonezawa, Shōnai, Morioka, Nagaoka in northern Honshu are the core of the Northern Alliance (pro-bakufu (幕府)) — visible as a band of medium-sized domains.
The Edo (modern Tokyo) city marker is mid-Honshu; Kyoto and Osaka are to the west of it.
The largest single domain in green near the centre-north is Kaga (加賀藩), the Maeda house, with kokudaka over 1.3 million koku — the largest non-Tokugawa daimyō in all of Japan.

title: 13  The 286 domains
text:
13.4 Why 286 domains and not, say, 270?
The “exactly N daimyō” count varies by source because the definition shifts:

In 1864 (mid-Edo): ~265 daimyō with productive fiefs
In 1868 (Restoration year): ~280
In 1870 (Hansei Ichiran): Adds post-Boshin reductions/relocations (e.g., 斗南藩 carved from reduced Aizu) — pushing the count up
In 1871 (haihan chiken (廃藩置県)): All abolished. The final count just before abolition was about 281.

The project’s 286 includes: - All 281 domains existing at haihan chiken (1871) - 2 Tokugawa sankyō branches (sankyō: Tayasu and Hitotsubashi) — hatamoto-rank but had administrative existence (the third sankyō line, Shimizu, had gone extinct by 1870) - 3 post-1869 special cases that Hansei Ichiran records as their own entries (e.g., 鶴田藩, carved from a former Aizu sub-fief)
An earlier version of the dataset listed 287 rows. One was a cross-volume duplicate: Hansei Ichiran writes the name of the Honjō domain (Dewa province, the Rokugō (六郷) family) as 本庄藩 in volume 1 and 本荘藩 in volume 2, and the two spellings were first recorded as separate rows. They are one domain, now merged into a single row — which gives the corrected count of 286.
A second, subtler duplicate was caught later (2026-05-30): Hansei Ichiran records Shōnai under its 1869 name 大泉藩 (120,000 koku, the Sakai family), and a separate “庄内藩” row had been created from an entry mis-read as 鶴岡藩 — which was in fact 邨岡藩 (村岡藩, Muraoka), an unrelated 11,000-koku tozama domain of the Yamana family in Tajima. De-duplicating Shōnai (now the single canonical 庄内藩) and restoring 村岡藩 as its own row left the total unchanged at 286. See Data Quality.






NoteWhy this granularity matters



A study that uses prefecture-level (47 prefectures, post-1871) data loses all variation within the 286-domain set. A study that uses a 1864 daimyō dataset misses the post-Boshin reductions that are crucial for understanding which families fell from rank vs. stayed. Hansei Ichiran’s 1870 timing — between Restoration and abolition — is uniquely informative.

title: 13  The 286 domains
section: 13.5 What’s NOT in the dataset (by design)
text:
13.5 What’s NOT in the dataset (by design)

tenryō (天領) — bakufu direct lands. These were governed by daikan (intendants), not daimyō. They became Meiji prefectures directly. The project includes one symbolic entry (大山領 = the Daisen-ji temple territory) for documentation but excludes them from analysis.
kuge — kuge (公家), court nobility. Became kazoku (華族) in 1884 but on a different track (without revenue or military). The outcome variable (kazoku rank) is restricted to former-daimyō lines.
Rows with no 1884 peerage rank — two rows in the dataset carry no kazoku rank, and both are genuine exclusions: 堀江藩 (the Ōsawa family, denied peerage after its kokudaka claim was found falsified in the 1871 Mangoku jiken) and 大山領, the Daisen-ji temple territory — never a daimyō domain, so no peerage applied. (A row that once appeared to be a third exclusion, 本庄藩, turned out to be a cross-volume duplicate of 本荘藩 and has been merged — see “Why 286 domains” above.) Other kaieki (改易, forced confiscation) cases — 林家 of 請西藩, for one — fall outside the dataset entirely, their domains dissolved before the 1870 survey.


Map source: Domain polygons from Harvard CHGIS 1664 daimyō shapefile (an approximation — 1664 boundaries were generally stable through 1870 but some small domains changed). kokudaka data: Hansei Ichiran (1870) with primary-source fallback (Ōkuma papers, Meiji Shiyō). Per-column citation in data_provenance.md.

title: 14  1664 → 1871: how stable were domain boundaries?
text:
14.1 The 1664 baseline
The maps in this walkthrough use the Harvard CHGIS 1664 daimyō (大名) shapefile to draw each domain’s polygon. The dataset itself measures 1870 — six years before haihan chiken (廃藩置県). So there’s a 206-year gap between the polygon vintage and the data vintage. This chapter documents what changed between those dates, what didn’t, and how the project handles each case.
By 1664, sixty-four years after Sekigahara, the Tokugawa daimyō system had largely stabilized. Major redistribution of fiefs (the kunigae / 国替 system, where domains were administratively reassigned to different families) had slowed dramatically from the chaotic 1601–1640 period. The 1664 shapefile captures 206 daimyō polygons representing the stable mid-Edo settlement.
Most of these polygons remained the same physical territory through 1870 — even when the ruling family changed, the geographic boundaries usually didn’t. This is what makes the 1664 shapefile usable as an 1870-era basemap for most cases.
crumbs: 14  1664 → 1871: how stable were domain boundaries?

For these reduced-territory cases, the polygon shown on the map is the 1664 boundary — i.e., larger than the actual 1870 territory. The data values (kokudaka, population, etc.) are correctly the post-Boshin 1870 figures. So when you look at, say, 斗南藩 on the map, the colored polygon visually represents the historic 230,000-koku Aizu territory, while the recorded kokudaka of 30,000 koku reflects the actual reduced Tonami territory.
This is the cleanest documented approximation the project makes. For statistical analysis the kokudaka value enters directly with no issue. For visual interpretation of “size on map,” there’s a mismatch in these ~6 cases.




14.2.3 Category 3 — Mid-Edo transfers (家替, iegae) where polygon = wrong family
A small number of domains had a family transfer that’s not captured in the 1664 shapefile because the transfer happened AFTER 1664. These are minor in number but the lookup table handles them:

倉敷藩 — became a tenryō (bakufu direct land) after a Bakumatsu-era confiscation
A handful of small domains created/abolished between 1664 and 1870 (e.g., post-Boshin 真鶴藩 wasn’t extant in 1664; 岩村田藩 changed families)

For these, the polygon may show the 1664 family / territory while the 1870 data is for the current family. The domain_name_crossref.csv lookup table bridges these, linking the polygon-side name to the data-side canonical.


14.2.4 Category 4 — Post-Boshin new domains (no 1664 polygon at all)
A handful of 1870 domains were created during 1868-69 and have no 1664 polygon:








1870 domain
Why it didn’t exist in 1664
1870 size




斗南藩

For these, the polygon-to-canonical lookup table either points to the pre-reduction polygon (e.g., 斗南 → uses 会津 polygon, accepting the size mismatch documented in Category 2) or leaves the polygon unshaded and notes the unmatched canonical.
Of the 286 canonicals in analytic_wide.csv, 171 match a 1664 polygon. The remaining ~115 either don’t match (small post-Boshin domains, sub-han, or sankyō (御三卿) without territory) or are minor name variants that didn’t get the alias-table treatment yet.


14.2.5 Category 5 — Sub-han (支藩) — present in 1664, sometimes missing from 1870 data
About 30 domains in 1664 were sub-han of larger parent domains — they had nominal daimyō status but were administratively dependent. Some examples: 大聖寺藩 (Maeda sub-han of Kaga), 富山藩 (another Maeda sub-han), 蓮池藩 (Nabeshima sub-han of Saga), 鹿島藩 (another Nabeshima sub-han), 小城藩 (also Nabeshima), 佐土原藩 (Shimazu sub-han).
By 1870, most sub-han had their own separate listing in Hansei Ichiran — but the 1664 shapefile may show them as carved out within a larger parent polygon, or sometimes as their own polygon. The mismatch is minor; visual interpretation usually doesn’t suffer.
crumbs: 14  1664 → 1871: how stable were domain boundaries?

title: 14  1664 → 1871: how stable were domain boundaries?
text:
14.3 The 1871 endpoint — haihan chiken
On August 29, 1871 (Meiji 4, 7th month 14th day), the Meiji government issued the haihan chiken edict abolishing all 280+ daimyō domains in a single proclamation. Every former daimyō became a governor (知事 chiji) of a corresponding prefecture (県 ken), initially with the same boundaries — but within months, these were consolidated into 72 prefectures, then 47 by 1888.
So by late 1871, every polygon on our map ceased to represent a political unit. The daimyō families became kazoku (華族) in 1884 (the paper’s outcome variable), but the land was thereafter administered by appointed prefectural governors under the central government.
crumbs: 14  1664 → 1871: how stable were domain boundaries?

title: 14  1664 → 1871: how stable were domain boundaries?
section: 14.4 What the blank white space on the maps means
text:
14.4 What the blank white space on the maps means
A reader looking at any of the choropleth maps notices that large stretches of Japan — especially through central Honshū around Kōfu and Hida — are left blank white, with no polygon at all. This is not missing data. It is land that was not a daimyō domain.
The base map contains polygons only for the 206 daimyō domains the 1664 shapefile captures. Roughly a quarter of Edo-period Japan, measured by assessed yield, was not daimyō domain and therefore has no polygon to draw:

天領 (tenryō) — land administered directly by the Tokugawa shogunate, about 4 million koku, scattered nationwide but concentrated around Edo, Kyoto, Osaka, Nagasaki, the mining districts, and — most visibly on the map — the provinces of Kai (the Kōfu basin) and Hida. The large white expanse through central Honshū is mostly tenryō.
旗本 (hatamoto) holdings — fiefs of the shogun’s direct bannermen, about 3 million koku, densely packed across the Kantō and Tōkai plains. Each individual hatamoto fief sat below the 10,000-koku threshold that defined a daimyō, so none of them counts as a “domain.”
寺社領 / 公家領 — temple, shrine, and imperial-court lands, smaller and concentrated around Kyoto and Nara.

None of that land is in a 286-domain dataset, by definition — the paper is about daimyō, and these lands had no daimyō. So they correctly show as white.
A second, smaller contributor: the 1664 shapefile has 206 polygons but the dataset has 286 domains. The ~115 domains without a 1664 polygon (small post-Boshin creations, sub-han, the landless sankyō) cannot be drawn, and contribute some additional white space where their territory would be.






NoteWhite vs grey — they mean different things




White = not a daimyō domain (shogunal tenryō, hatamoto land, temple / court land, or an unmapped small domain). No polygon exists.
Grey = this is a daimyō domain with a polygon, but it has no value for the specific variable the map is colouring.

The white space is a feature of the historical map, not a defect in the data. It shows at a glance how much of Japan the shogunate and its direct vassals controlled — the daimyō domains, for all their political weight, were a patchwork around a large shogunal core.
crumbs: 14  1664 → 1871: how stable were domain boundaries?

title: 14  1664 → 1871: how stable were domain boundaries?
text:
14.5 Implications for the maps

Polygons are 1664 vintage — used as a geographic approximation of the 1870 stable territory
Reduced post-Boshin cases (~6 domains) have polygons larger than their 1870 actual territory; documented above
Renamed cases (~10 domains) match correctly via alias lookup; no territorial issue
Post-Boshin-created domains (~5) reuse the parent’s polygon with the size-mismatch caveat
For statistical analysis, the polygons don’t enter — only the data values do, and those are 1870-correct
For visual interpretation, the dominant pattern (Kyushu = pro-Restoration coalition, Tōhoku = Northern Alliance, etc.) is unaffected by these approximations; only the size-scale on a few specific reduced-territory cases is misleading







NoteCould the shogunate land be drawn in, instead of left white?



Not easily, and the reason is historically substantive. Shogunal land (shihaisho 支配所 in the period; tenryō 天領 is the Meiji-era retronym) was not contiguous territory. Unlike a daimyō domain — a mostly-connected block — shogunal land was a scatter of individual villages and districts across the whole country, and hatamoto bannermen holdings were more fragmented still. There is no clean polygon to draw, because the thing itself was not a clean polygon.
The CHGIS daimyō shapefile this project uses is, by design, daimyō-only — its documentation lists a single topic, “dmyo.” It has no shogunal-land layer, and the same is true of the other off-the-shelf historical-Japan GIS products.
A correct delineation of shogunal land has to be built from the village level up. Two near-comprehensive shogunal land surveys exist — one for 1664, one for ca. 1868 (the 旧高旧領取調帳, Kyūdaka kyūryō torishirabe-chō) — and the standard approach is to geocode every village in one of those surveys and colour it by holder type. The 1664 survey was mapped this way by Nishioka & Hattori (1956) and by Drixler (2015). The ongoing Digital Atlas of Tokugawa Japan (Fabian Drixler, Yale) is reconstructing the entire territorial structure village-by-village; its geospatial output is promised as a free web resource but is not yet a downloadable shapefile.
So: drawing the shogunate’s land would be a genuine GIS project (acquire or build village-level holder data, then aggregate), not a drop-in basemap swap. For this walkthrough the white space, explained above, is the honest and standard treatment — it accurately reads as “the non-daimyō remainder.” Separately, CHGIS does have 1828 and 1869 daimyō snapshots (this project used only the 1664 one); swapping in the 1869 snapshot would improve the daimyō side, capturing post-Boshin reductions, without addressing the shogunal land.



Polygon source: Harvard China Historical GIS (CHGIS) 1664 daimyō shapefile. Territorial reduction details: 工藤威『奥羽列藩同盟の基礎的研究 (Ōu reppan dōmei no kisoteki kenkyū, “Basic Research on the Northern Alliance”)』(岩田書院 2002) for Northern Alliance members; Lebra (1993) and Asami (1994) for the rank-vs-territory distinction; per-domain JP Wikipedia articles for individual rename / transfer specifics. Haihan-chiken 1871: Beasley (1972) Ch. XII; 「haihan chikenの詔書」, dajōkan-fu, 1871-07-14 (lunar).

title: 15  shinpan / fudai / tozama — the Tokugawa political classification
text:
15.1 The four categories — definitions and origins
The previous chapters of this walkthrough have used the Tokugawa-era category (shinpan / fudai / tozama / sankyō / tenryō (天領)) as a control variable without formally introducing it. This chapter fills that gap: where the classification comes from, what each category means in practice, how the 286 domains in the dataset distribute across the categories, and how the categories interact with the 1884 kazoku (華族) rank outcomes.
crumbs: 15  *shinpan* / *fudai* / *tozama* — the Tokugawa political classification

title: 15  shinpan / fudai / tozama — the Tokugawa political classification
text:
15.1.1 shinpan (親藩) — Tokugawa cadets
Houses founded by sons or close male relatives of Tokugawa Ieyasu and continued as separate domains. The category was deliberately constructed by Ieyasu and his successors to ensure that, if the main Tokugawa line failed to produce an heir, an unambiguous chain of succession existed within the Tokugawa family itself.

The three flagship shinpan houses are the gosanke (御三家) (Owari-Tokugawa, Kii-Tokugawa, Mito-Tokugawa), established by three of Ieyasu’s sons. Each got a major domain (Nagoya, Wakayama, Mito).
The next tier is the 越前松平 lineage (Echizen-Matsudaira), descended from Ieyasu’s second son 結城秀康 — Fukui is the senior line. The Echizen-Matsudaira branches multiplied through the Tokugawa period and supplied many smaller shinpan.
A separate branch is the 久松松平 lineage (Hisamatsu-Matsudaira), descended from Ieyasu’s half-brother through his mother’s remarriage. Major Hisamatsu-Matsudaira domains include Iyo-Matsuyama, Imabari, and Kuwana (the latter reclassified to shinpan; see the methodology callout below).
The sankyō (Hitotsubashi, Tayasu, Shimizu) — three later 18th-century lines from sons of Yoshimune and Ieshige — are a separate sub-category (see chapter 02h).



15.1.2 fudai (譜代) — long-standing vassals
Houses whose ancestors served the Tokugawa family before Sekigahara (1600) as vassals — many of them from the Mikawa heartland where the Matsudaira/Tokugawa originated. The fudai were the political backbone of the bakufu administration: only fudai could hold the senior offices of 老中 (rōjū) and 若年寄 (wakadoshiyori), making them the operating elite.
In compensation for their political reliability, fudai were typically given smaller domains — Tokugawa policy explicitly limited them to moderate territories on the assumption that their administrative power, not their land base, was their real reward. Hence the strikingly skewed kokudaka (石高) distribution: most fudai are concentrated at 10,000–50,000 koku, with only a few crossing 100,000.
Within fudai, key sub-tiers include the 大老 (tairō) houses (Ii of Hikone, Sakai of Shōnai, etc., who supplied the highest bakufu post on rotation), standard fudai (Sakai, Honda, Mizuno, Itakura, Toda, Mōri-of-Saiki, etc.), and late-elevation fudai (旗本 promoted to daimyō status in the 18th-19th centuries).


15.1.3 tozama (外様) — outside vassals
Houses who only submitted to the Tokugawa at or after Sekigahara (1600). The category includes most of the major regional powers of Sengoku Japan — Shimazu in Satsuma, Mōri in Chōshū, Date in Sendai, Maeda in Kaga, Yamauchi in Tosa, Kuroda in Fukuoka, Hosokawa in Kumamoto, Nabeshima in Saga.
External-vassal status carried a double-edged meaning: tozama were typically allowed to keep large territories (because their submission was the price of peace, and reducing them was politically expensive), but they were structurally excluded from the inner bakufu administration. The standard sankin kōtai treatment was harsher for tozama, the bakufu maintained closer surveillance of them, and any sign of disloyalty would be met more aggressively (the most spectacular Edo-era reductions were of tozama houses).
The bakufu’s strategic geography placed tozama mostly in outer Japan (Kyushu, Shikoku, the Japan Sea coast, the north) — far from the political centre. The closer tozama (e.g., the eastern Date branches, Ueda) were the exception.


15.1.4 sankyō (御三卿) — hatamoto-class Tokugawa relatives
A small mid-18th-century innovation: three branch houses (Hitotsubashi, Tayasu, Shimizu) of the shogunal family, with no domain but with stipends and ceremonial status. Their political function was to serve as an heir pool for the shōgun (将軍). By 1870 only Hitotsubashi and Tayasu were still extant (Shimizu had become extinct and was only revived in 1895), and these two are the sankyō rows in the dataset. See the Sankyō walkthrough for the full discussion.


15.1.5 tenryō — bakufu direct holdings
Lands held directly by the bakufu, administered by appointed daikan (代官) rather than by an inheritable daimyō family. By 1870 most former tenryō had been reorganised into 府 / 県 administrative units; the dataset contains one residual tenryō row for completeness.

The 112 anti-bakufu fudai look counterintuitive — fudai were the bakufu’s political backbone, so we might expect them to defend the bakufu in the Boshin War (Boshin sensō). The puzzle resolves on close inspection:

Most “anti-bakufu” fudai are coded as such by the “post-Toba-Fushimi imperial consolidation” rule (Beasley 1972, ch. XI). After the bakufu’s military defeat at Toba-Fushimi (January 1868), the imperial side issued a general directive to all daimyō to switch sides; most small fudai complied without resistance. This is end-state alignment, not active anti-bakufu combat.
The actively-anti-bakufu fudai are a much smaller subset — Hikone (彦根 Ii, defected pre-Toba-Fushimi), Yodo (淀, castle-gate refusal at Toba-Fushimi 1868-01-28), and a few others.
The 22 pro-bakufu fudai are the actually-loyal core — Sendai-affiliated, Aizu-affiliated, members of the Northern Alliance who fought on the pro-bakufu side, etc.

The shinpan split (21 anti / 6 pro) is similarly explained — most shinpan either defected or simply complied with the imperial directive; the 6 pro-bakufu shinpan are the actively-loyal Tokugawa-cadet branches (Aizu/斗南, Kuwana, Iyo-Matsuyama, Shimousa-Tsuruta, Mito itself by family-affiliation, Shizuoka).
The tozama split (102 anti / 11 pro) is the cleanest: most tozama were on the Restoration side, and the 11 pro-bakufu tozama are the surprise cases — typically smaller eastern tozama or tozama whose specific lord supported the bakufu against the regional pattern.

title: 15  shinpan / fudai / tozama — the Tokugawa political classification
text:
15.7 Why fudai cluster at viscount
The rank cluster pattern — 118 of 137 fudai (~86%) at viscount, 0 above count — is mostly mechanical:

fudai median genmai is ~10,000 koku (well below the 50,000-koku count threshold).
The 1884 rule placed everything below 50,000 at viscount by default.
The 13 count-rank fudai are exactly the fudai whose genmai cleared 50,000 — Hikone, Himeji, Kōriyama, Toyotsu, Mito’s surrounding loyalists, etc.

The 5 baron-rank fudai are the post-1868 fudai elevations: 今尾 (Owari 付家老), 新宮/田辺 (Kii 付家老), 松岡 (Mito 付家老), and 田原本 (旗本 elevation). See chapter 07 §D for full per-family narratives.

15.8 Four mis-classifications corrected during sourcing review






TipMethodology note



The dataset’s domain_category column was initially seeded from a web-scraped Bakumatsu-era domain index. Every row was subsequently re-cited against published academic sources (児玉幸多 ed.『藩史総覧 (Hanshi sōran, “Comprehensive Survey of Han History”)』新人物往来社 1977; Beasley 1972; Japanese Wikipedia shinpan / 譜代大名 / 外様大名 / sankyō articles used as a finder only). The re-sourcing surfaced four mis-classifications, now corrected:

The first three corrections came from cross-checking against Beasley (1972) p. xii table of principal domains in 1860 (which explicitly classifies Date and Sō as tozama). The Kuwana correction came from the Hisamatsu-Matsudaira lineage tracing — since 伊予松山 and 今治 (both Hisamatsu-Matsudaira) were already coded shinpan, consistency requires Kuwana (also Hisamatsu-Matsudaira) be shinpan as well.
These mis-classifications matter for the paper because they appear in cross-tabs where category is a regressor. The final distribution in this chapter — shinpan 29 / fudai 137 / tozama 117 / sankyō 2 / tenryō 1 — reflects the corrected values.

title: 15  shinpan / fudai / tozama — the Tokugawa political classification
section: 15.9 What this teaches about the paper’s empirical claim
text:
15.9 What this teaches about the paper’s empirical claim
Three lessons from the cross-tabs:

Category and rank are mechanically linked through the genmai threshold — fudai cluster at viscount because their genmai is small. This is a structural link, not a discretionary one.
Category and Boshin alignment are weakly linked in the data because most domains followed the post-Toba-Fushimi imperial directive. The active-combatant subset is small in every category.
The interaction of category × Boshin × rank is most informative in the deviation sample (chapter 07) — the 19 families whose 1884 rank diverged from genmai-mechanical prediction split by category as: 3 shinpan deviations (Shizuoka, Mito, Fukui), 5 tozama upgrades + 2 tozama downgrades, and 5 fudai downgrades. The category effect is real but operates through specific rule provisions (the sanke clause, the Tokugawa-main clause, the post-1868-elevation provision), not through general “shinpan are favoured” or “tozama are punished” patterns.


Citation chain for the Tokugawa-era category column:
Primary scholarly reference: 児玉幸多 ed.『藩史総覧』新人物往来社 (1977) — canonical han-classification compendium covering all ~300 domains with formal category designations.
Definitive secondary: Kokushi daijiten (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1979–1997) — the standard reference work for individual house histories.
English source for major domains: Beasley, W.G. The Meiji Restoration. Stanford University Press (1972), p. xii — “Principal Domains in 1860” table with explicit shinpan/fudai/tozama designations.
Cross-validation finder: JP Wikipedia shinpan / 譜代大名 / 外様大名 / sankyō articles, used as a quick lookup for the rare disputed cases.
Hisamatsu-Matsudaira convention for the Kuwana reclassification: 中川 学『松平氏由緒書 (Matsudaira-shi yuishogaki, “Matsudaira Lineage Records”)』(Mikawa Edo bunko 1989); JP Wikipedia 久松松平家 article.
The full per-row citation lives in the domain_category_source column of the dataset, and the audit of the four corrections accompanies the dataset release.

NoteThe several kokudaka — and which one the paper uses



A reader can reasonably ask why the revenue row lists five fields. They belong to two families:

Assessed yield — what the land was rated to produce. The Tokugawa cadastral surveys rated every domain in koku, and Hansei Ichiran reports that rating in several variants. The two that matter conceptually are omotedaka (表高) — the “face” figure registered with the shogunate, frozen at early-1600s levels, which fixed a domain’s rank, precedence, and military obligation — and naidaka (内高), the domain’s own internal figure from its later surveys, which by the 1860s usually ran well above the official omotedaka. Sōdaka (草高), gōdaka (合高 — gross plus reclaimed new fields), and gendaka (現高) are further variants of this assessed-capacity number.
Actual rice — genmai (現米): the rice that actually flowed as tax in 1869. Because a domain taxed only a fraction of its assessed yield, genmai runs far below any assessed figure.

The paper uses both, for different jobs. The composite kokudaka (kokudaka_iv), drawn from the assessed-yield family, is the paper’s predictor — its measure of a domain’s size and revenue base. Genmai is used only to compute the expected 1884 peerage rank under the Asami (1994) threshold rule. They are not competing measures of one quantity.
The gap between the official omotedaka and a domain’s real productive base is a standard theme of early-modern Japanese fiscal history (see Ravina 1999). The kokudaka-versus-genmai question as it bears on the peerage is the Asami-versus-Lebra debate walked through in the kazoku-rank chapter and the 19-deviations chapter.

title: 16  The variables
section: 16.2 The most important columns for the paper
text:
16.2 The most important columns for the paper

16.2.1 Revenue base — kokudaka (column kokudaka_iv)
The composite kokudaka — the maximum of the available Hansei Ichiran fields (sōdaka, gōdaka, omotedaka), then Kyūdaka kyūryō torishirabechō (旧高旧領取調帳, 1872 Meiji cadastral survey, hosted by the National Museum of Japanese History / Rekihaku) for the 13 domains absent from HI, then the Wikipedia omotedaka fallback for any remaining gaps. This is the denominator in the paper’s headline retainers-per-1,000-koku fiscal-distress metric (see chapter 04b).

Coverage: 276/286
Range: ~2,000 to ~1,400,000 koku
Why composite: Hansei Ichiran reports different fields for different domains. Saga uses 貮口合 (= gōdaka); Chōshū uses sōdaka; some small domains only have omotedaka. The composite picks the most-comprehensive figure available.
The Rekihaku tier (added 2026-05-24): 13 domains have no recordable kokudaka in HI vol 1 (仙台, 新発田, 杵築, 母里, 清崎, 足利, 須坂, 岩村, 対馬府中, 岩国, 岩崎, 松岡, 斗南). For these, the IV is now sourced from the 1872 Kyūdaka kyūryō torishirabechō cadastral survey — summed per-village from the Rekihaku open-access database. This is a kusadaka-tier (actual rated land yield) metric, the same conceptual tier as HI sōdaka / gōdaka, restoring methodological consistency. For 仙台 specifically, the IV uses only post-Boshin retained territory (excluding 1869 carve-off prefectures 登米/胆沢/江刺/角田), giving 299,659 koku instead of the raw 974,010-koku all-territory figure. See CORRECTIONS_LOG.md Section K for the full methodological discussion.
Citation: a sibling source column records which field was actually used for each domain (hi_sodaka, hi_godaka, hi_omotedaka, rekihaku_kyudaka_1872, wiki_omotedaka).







NoteThe Sendai-Date case in detail



仙台藩 is the one case in the dataset where the pre-Boshin and at-haihan-chiken kokudaka diverge dramatically. The 1869 Boshin punishment was a full territorial confiscation followed by re-grant of 28万石 to the three-year-old heir Date Munemoto 伊達宗基 (Meiji 1/12/12 = 1869-01-24, per 仙台市史 通史編6 近代1, 2008; 栗原伸一郎『戊辰戦争と「奥羽越」列藩同盟』, 2017; Bakkalian, The Sparrow’s Dream, Pittsburgh PhD 2017).
This shows up in Hansei Ichiran itself: vol 2 p.116R carries an explicit 「草高等不詳細」 (“kokudaka and similar details unclear”) annotation directly under the ○仙臺藩 header. HI itself documented being unable to record Sendai’s kokudaka in 1870, almost certainly because the new post-Boshin cadastral was incomplete at the time of the survey. This is a much stronger provenance for using a substitute kokudaka than the prior “absent from HI” framing.
The dataset carries four Sendai kokudaka tiers for transparency:

kokudaka_iv (the paper’s IV)
