The carved-off territory (974,010 − 299,659 = 674,350 koku, in the 1869-created 登米県 / 胆沢県 / 江刺県 / 角田県) matches the documented Sendai 62万→28万 punitive reduction almost exactly — the 1872 cadastral preserves the Boshin penalty’s territorial fingerprint via the 旧県 column.
A methodological caveat: the 旧領 column in 旧高旧領取調帳 uses different temporal-reference conventions across prefectures (per Rekihaku’s own documentation and Kimura Motoi 校訂, 1969-79). The Tōhoku volumes (where Sendai sits) treat 旧領 as the pre-Boshin Bakumatsu lord — so the carve-off filter is correct for Sendai but the convention should be re-verified for other regions before being applied elsewhere.

Coverage: 284/286 (2 documented exclusions)

Each domain’s side in the Boshin War (Boshin sensō).

Coverage: 286/286
Values: anti-bakufu (幕府) / pro-restoration, pro-bakufu, neutral
Distribution: 235 anti-bakufu, 41 pro-bakufu, 10 neutral
Citation chain: Esposito (2020) + 工藤威 (2002) + Beasley (1972). See the Boshin Alignment chapter.



16.2.4 Paper-relevant — Genmai-rule deviations (column kazoku_rank_deviation_genmai)
The 19 daimyō (大名) families whose actual 1884 rank diverged from the mechanical prediction.

Coverage: 19/286 (most domains land where the rule predicts)
Why this column exists: Asami (1994) argues the 1884 threshold operated on genmai (現米) — post-1869 tax-reported rice flow — not sōdaka/omotedaka. Applying the genmai rule, 19 cases show real deviations. These are the paper’s core sample.
See The 19 Deviations chapter.



16.2.5 Paper-relevant — Focal-han tier (columns is_focal_han_tier1, is_focal_han_tier2, focal_han_tier_source)
The two-tier focal-domain commendation issued by the haihan-chiken imperial decree on 1871/7/14.

Tier 1 (4 domains, is_focal_han_tier1): Sat-Chō-Saga-Tosa = 薩摩 / 長州 / 佐賀 / 土佐 — the four domains explicitly commended for having first proposed the 1869 hanseki hōkan (return of registers). Synonymous with is_focal_han (both columns kept for naming clarity).
Tier 2 (8 domains, is_focal_han_tier2): Tier 1 plus 尾張 (Owari/Nagoya, Tokugawa) + 鳥取 (Tottori, Ikeda) + 熊本 (Kumamoto, Hosokawa) + 徳島 (Tokushima, Hachisuka) — the four additional domains commended on the same day for implementation merit (hanseki ritsu-sei, establishment of the [post-Restoration] register system).
Why this column exists: the project’s reading of the haihan-chiken-day primary sources documents two tiers of imperial commendation, not just one. The eight Tier-2 domains together represent the political coalition that publicly underwrote the abolition — useful as an alternative control variable for analyses where the four-domain Sat-Chō-Saga-Tosa indicator might be too narrow.
Coverage: 100% (binary indicators populated for every domain). Tier 1 count = 4; Tier 2 count = 8.
Citation chain (per focal_han_tier_source): triple-confirmed by the haihan-chiken imperial decree (Meiji Shiyō vol 1 pp.27–28), Inoue Kaoru’s biography (vol 1 p.452, which gives the explicit list 熊本・名古屋・徳島・鳥取), and Kido Takayoshi’s diary (vol II p.65, 28–29 Aug 1871 entries, in the Brown & Hirota 1983 translation). Project verbatim transcriptions: haihan_chiken_decree_meijishiyo_vol1.md, inoue_kaoru_haihan_chiken_account.md, and kido_takayoshi_haihan_chiken_diary.md. A fourth-source corroboration from Ōkubo Toshimichi’s contemporaneous diary + the 1928 editorial 【辨説】 commentary in the Ōkubo Monjo vol 4 is at okubo_toshimichi_haihan_chiken_account.md.
Note on Ikeda ambiguity: Kido’s diary mentions “the Governors Ikeda” without specifying Tottori versus Okayama (both Ikeda lines). Inoue’s biography is explicit on Tottori (鳥取), which the project treats as definitive.

title: 17  Fiscal distress: the retainer burden
text:
17.1 The metric: retainers per 1,000 koku
The paper’s central use of Hansei Ichiran is not to predict who got which 1884 peerage rank. It is to document the structural fiscal crisis that made the 1871 abolition of the feudal domains (haihan chiken (廃藩置県)) a mutually advantageous bargain in the first place.
The Political Coase Theorem argument requires that both parties to a bargain perceive the gains from cooperation to exceed the costs of conflict. For the new Meiji government, the gain was monopoly over taxation and the military. For the daimyō (大名) — the roughly 280 feudal lords — the gain had to be large enough to outweigh their reluctance to surrender three centuries of inherited sovereignty. Hansei Ichiran lets us see why that gain was, in fact, large: by 1870 most domains were insolvent.
The headline statistic the paper uses is retainers per 1,000 koku — the number of hereditary samurai (侍) a domain owed stipends to, normalised by the domain’s revenue base (its kokudaka (石高)). It functions as a standardised measure of structural insolvency: how heavily a domain’s fixed, hereditary payroll obligations weighed on its productive capacity.
A higher ratio means more samurai mouths to feed for each unit of economic base — and because samurai stipends were a hereditary right chitsuroku (秩禄), not a discretionary expense the lord could trim, the ratio is closer to a fixed liability than a flexible cost. A domain at 100 retainers per 1,000 koku has roughly twice the per-koku stipend burden of one at 50.
The numerator is the count of samurai-class persons living off domain stipends (combining shizoku (士族), the regular samurai class — and sotsu (卒族), the foot-rank retainers). The denominator is the composite kokudaka from Hansei Ichiran, in thousands of koku.

In a previous version of this chapter Tsushima appeared at rank 7 (288.7 retainers per 1,000 koku, kokudaka 52,174). The substantive finding is unchanged: Tsushima was structurally distressed — its retainer burden remained one of the dozen highest in the dataset. The change reflects switching from a frozen formal title to actual cadastral assessment.

title: 17  Fiscal distress: the retainer burden
section: 17.5 What this implies for the bargain
text:
17.5 What this implies for the bargain
By 1870, the typical daimyō was paying out hereditary stipends to ~5 percent of their formal koku base — a fixed obligation with no realistic prospect of relief through ordinary fiscal management. The 1871 haihan chiken offered each lord:

Debt relief. The state assumed the domain’s accumulated debts to merchants and rice-brokers.
A personal stipend of one-tenth of the domain’s revenue, paid by the central government (later converted to interest-bearing kinroku kōsai (金禄公債) bonds in the 1876 chitsuroku shobun (秩禄処分) — terms detailed in the project’s transcription of 太政官布告 第108号: regressive multipliers × progressive interest, 5-year grace + 30-year lottery amortization; Sat-Chō-Saga-Tosa former daimyō all sit in the least-generous conversion tier).
kazoku peerage status, eventually formalised as the 1884 five-tier system (see the project transcription of Kanpō #307).
Access to government office. The largest domains received sangi (参議, Imperial Councilor) appointments; the rest received genrōin (元老院, Senate of Elders) membership and other formal positions.

Set against the alternative — continuing to service a hereditary stipend payroll worth a third of national revenue, with no monopoly cash crops, no tax-collection apparatus, and the new Imperial Army next door — the bargain was lopsided in favour of acceptance. Only one domain head (島津久光, the Satsuma 国父) seriously objected, and as the paper notes (Ravina 2003), his retainer 大久保利通 had already taken the matter out of his hands.
That receptiveness is what Hansei Ichiran documents. The 1870 survey was not commissioned to predict 1884 peerage assignments. It was commissioned because the new government needed to know — in precise, per-domain detail — exactly how unsustainable the existing system was, so that the buyout could be priced and the political bargain could be made.

Citation chain for this chapter:
Primary data: Hansei Ichiran (藩制一覧), 1870, both volumes, NDL Digital Collections. Per-domain shizoku and sotsu person counts; per-domain composite kokudaka.
Supplementary primary: Meiji Shiyō, the Ōkuma 府藩県石高人口表 (Fu-han-ken kokudaka jinkō-hyō, “Prefecture-Domain-County Kokudaka and Population Table”) (Waseda Library イ14-A4361), and Tōkei Shūshi 人員表 (Jin’in-hyō, “Personnel Table”) for cross-validated population counts where Hansei Ichiran’s entries are partial.
Paper-published per-domain values: Reese & Crego (forthcoming), Historical Setting §2. Where the paper’s specific retainer counts diverge from the dataset’s shizoku+sotsu sum, the source is documented in the paper’s footnote 5 (ashigaru foot-soldiers excluded).
Methodological framing: Craig, A.M., Chōshū in the Meiji Restoration (Harvard University Press, 1961) for the 1840s 長州 reforms that explain its post-1870 lean position. Banno, Japan’s Modern History 1857–1937 (2010) and Gordon, A Modern History of Japan (2002) for the aggregate national stipend burden of 18.78 million yen / ~33 percent of state expenditure in 1875.

title: 18  kazoku (華族) rank — the institutional side payment
text:
18.1 Why a peerage at all?
In the paper’s Political Coase Theorem framing, the 1884 kazoku peerage system is the side payment that made the 1871 bargain credible. The daimyō (大名) had surrendered fiscal sovereignty in 1871. By 1884 they had been disarmed, relocated to Tokyo, and stripped of any practical means to resist the central state. The kazoku peerage gave the new arrangement a stable institutional shape: hereditary noble status, formally derived from the Emperor, that the state could not later default on without dismantling its own ceremonial architecture.
This chapter walks the kazoku system — what it was, how the five ranks were assigned, and how it functioned as a credible-commitment mechanism in the broader bargain.
The new Meiji state in 1871 faced a credible-commitment problem. It had just assumed responsibility for paying the hereditary stipends of every former-daimyō family and tens of thousands of their samurai (侍) retainers — an obligation that, by 1875, consumed nearly a third of national revenue (Banno 2010; Gordon 2002). It had every short-term incentive to default once the daimyō were powerless to enforce the bargain. The question was: why wouldn’t it?
Three mechanisms together provided the answer:
The 1884 ranks are step 2 in that sequence — the moment the loose kazoku status acquired its precise institutional shape.
crumbs: 18  *kazoku* (華族) rank — the institutional side payment

title: 18  kazoku (華族) rank — the institutional side payment
text:
The 1869 founding of the kazoku status merged the former-daimyō with the existing kuge (公家) court aristocracy into a single national peerage formally derived from the Emperor. Status became Emperor-granted, not domain-derived, anchoring it to the regime’s own legitimacy.
The 1884 kazoku Law (華族令) formalised the five hereditary ranks with explicit assignment rules, converting the loose 1869 status into a structured institutional class with documented inheritance.
The 1889 Meiji Constitution wrote the kazoku into the House of Peers (貴族院, kizoku-in) — the upper chamber of the new legislature. From 1889 onwards, defaulting on kazoku status meant dismantling part of the state’s own legislative architecture.

text:
18.3 The assignment rule
The 1884 law was vague on assignment; the internal 叙爵内規 (jōshaku naiki, “internal assignment rules”) supplied the quantitative thresholds:



Threshold
Rank




≥ 150,000 koku
Marquess


50,000 – 149,999 koku
Count


< 50,000 koku
Viscount


(Special — see below)
Prince



Most former-daimyō were assigned mechanically by their domain’s revenue base: large → marquess, medium → count, small → viscount.
Prince was reserved by specific clauses for:

The Tokugawa main line (徳川宗家) — Tokugawa-main-line exception
The five sekke (摂家) kuge court houses (近衛, 鷹司, 九条, 一条, 二条) — applies to court not daimyō
島津家 (Satsuma) and 毛利家 (Chōshū) — explicit Restoration-leader reward
A handful of later upgrades (e.g., 伊藤博文, 1907)

title: 18  kazoku (華族) rank — the institutional side payment
section: 18.4 The plot twist — the threshold operates on genmai, not sōdaka (草高)
text:
18.4 The plot twist — the threshold operates on genmai, not sōdaka (草高)
The threshold rule above is usually summarised with sōdaka (sōdaka, the nominal yield assessment). Under that reading, 仙台藩 (Sendai, the Date family) at ~620,000 koku should have got marquess but received count — and that gap was traditionally read as a Boshin punishment for joining the Northern Alliance.
Asami (1994) shows the threshold was actually applied to genmai (現米) — the post-1869 tax-reported actual rice flow, which is much smaller than sōdaka for most domains. Sendai’s genmai was ~67,740 koku — squarely in count range. By the genmai rule, Sendai’s rank is mechanical, not punishment. Aizu’s post-Boshin remnant 斗南藩 (Tonami) is similarly mechanical: its genmai of ~7,380 koku put it in viscount range. The territorial reduction of Aizu was the punishment; the rank assignment just followed.
This re-baseline matters for any descriptive interpretation of the 1884 rank pattern. Under the sōdaka reading, 93 domains look like deviations from the rule. Under the genmai reading, only 19 do — and those 19 fall into a clean set of named exception clauses (gosanke clause for Mito, Tokugawa-main clause for Shizuoka, Restoration-leader clause for Satsuma+Chōshū, post-1868-elevation rule for the eight 付家老 / 旗本 elevations). The data documents an institutional architecture, not a discretionary rewards-and-punishments scheme. The 19 cases are walked through in chapter 07.

title: 18  kazoku (華族) rank — the institutional side payment
text:
18.6 How to read this map

The purple cluster in Kyushu and at the western Honshu tip (薩摩, 長州) is the Restoration coalition that got the prince and marquess promotions.
The orange cluster in the north — 仙台, 米沢, 盛岡, the post-Boshin Aizu remnant 斗南 — is mostly former Northern Alliance members at count rank. Read together with the fiscal-distress data, the count rank for these houses is the side-payment side of the bargain: the post-Boshin territorial reductions had been the cost; the kazoku rank is the institutionalised compensation.
Light purple (marquess) is scattered — large tozama (加賀, 土佐, 佐賀, 熊本, 広島, 岡山, 福岡, 徳島, 秋田) plus the gosanke (尾張, 紀伊, 水戸).
Viscount is everywhere small — the 222 small-to-medium domains that cleared the threshold.
Brown (baron) is the 9 post-1868 elevations — former 付家老 (tsukegarō) and 旗本 (hatamoto) who became independent daimyō only at the Restoration. Their baron rank reflects pre-Restoration baishin (陪臣) status, not Boshin alignment.
crumbs: 18  *kazoku* (華族) rank — the institutional side payment

title: 18  kazoku (華族) rank — the institutional side payment
text:
18.7 The kazoku as credible commitment
The paper’s reading of the 1884 ranks is institutional, not predictive. The ranks themselves were the instrument of the political bargain — the visible, hereditary, Emperor-derived status that bound the former-daimyō families to the new regime’s success. By 1889 the constitutional architecture made this binding explicit: prince and marquess seats in the House of Peers were automatic and hereditary; count, viscount, and baron seats were elected from within each rank. The state could not default on kazoku status without simultaneously destroying its own upper chamber. That is the credible-commitment mechanism the paper identifies.

Primary record: Kanpō 第307号 (1884-07-08) and 第308号 (1884-07-09), NDL Digital Collections (pids 2943511 and 2943512) — the government gazettes publishing the ennoblement decree. Project verbatim transcription: the imperial decree text + 83-person merit-track ennoblement roster + 10 AM 1884-07-07 ceremony procedure are transcribed in primary_source_transcriptions/kazoku_rei_decree_kanpo_307.md. The transcription documents two findings worth noting here: (1) the decree’s dual rationale (lineage track + merit track) is encoded in its founding text; (2) all 16 senior-merit (勳一等) Counts on 1884-07-07 were drawn from the focal-4 han (7 Satsuma + 6 Chōshū + 2 Tosa + 1 Saga), confirming the focal-han coalition’s continuity from haihan-chiken (1871) into the kazoku-rei (1884).
Sendai-Date hybrid framing (new 2026-05-25): 從五位 伊達宗基 received Count rank in the Kanpō #307 roster as 「依父勳功特授伯爵」 — formally categorized as merit-track ennoblement inherited from his father’s service. This is a third interpretive frame for Sendai’s rank, complementing Lebra’s Boshin-penalty reading and Asami’s genmai-mechanical reading — see the 19 deviations chapter for discussion.
Scholarly synthesis: 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治 (Kazoku tanjō: meiyo to taimen no Meiji, “Birth of the Peerage: Honor and Reputation in Meiji”)』リブロポート (1994) — backs the assignment-rule reconstruction and the genmai / sōdaka distinction. 華族大鑑刊行会編『華族大鑑 (Kazoku taikan, “Compendium of the Peerage”)』(1990) — primary source for the prince-rank ennoblements. 小田部雄次『華族 近代日本貴族の虚像と実像 (Kazoku: kindai Nihon kizoku no kyozō to jitsuzō, “The Peerage: Image and Reality of the Modern Japanese Aristocracy”)』中公新書 (2006) — secondary kazoku reference. 松田敬之『〈華族爵位〉請願人名辞典 (Kazoku shakui seigan jinmei jiten, “Dictionary of Peerage-Title Petitioners”)』吉川弘文館 (2015) — definitive on 1884 exclusions.
Actor perspective on chitsuroku→kazoku-rei sequencing: Kido Takayoshi’s contemporaneous diary records his August 1876 opposition to chitsuroku-shobun timing and Itō Hirobumi’s overruling. The diary explicitly attributes the 8-year gap (1876 → 1884) between chitsuroku-shobun and kazoku-rei to Itō’s “political tactics” calculus. Project transcription: kido_takayoshi_chitsuroku_shobun_diary.md. The chitsuroku-shobun decree itself: chitsuroku_shobun_decree_1876.md.
Institutional architecture: Beckmann (1957) on the 1889 Constitution and the House of Peers. Beasley, The Meiji Restoration (1972), ch. XI.

title: 19  Boshin alignment (the paper’s treatment variable)
text:
19.1 The two coalitions
The Boshin War (戊辰戦争, Boshin sensō, 1868–69) was the civil war that ended the Tokugawa bakufu (幕府, the shōgunate). Every daimyō (大名) family had to take a side. Sixteen years later, those choices mattered for the kazoku (華族) rank they received.

Led by:

Kuwana (桑名藩) — Hisamatsu-Matsudaira

Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei (奥羽越列藩同盟) — the Northern Alliance:

31 northern domains, led by Sendai (仙台) + Aizu
Including: Yonezawa, Shōnai, Morioka, Nagaoka, Nihonmatsu, Hachinohe, Iwakidaira, others
Several defected to Imperial side mid-war (Akita, Hirosaki, Matsumae, Shinjō, Honjō, Shibata, Moriyama, Yashima)

Neutral: 10 domains (incl. Kaga — the famously cautious Maeda house).

title: 20  Patterns in the 1884 rank assignments
text:
20.1 Why are there deviations at all?
This chapter documents a specific pattern in the dataset: 19 cases where the 1884 kazoku (華族) peerage rank a family received departed from what the 叙爵内規 (assignment rules) would have predicted from their domain’s revenue base alone. These 19 cases are not the paper’s central empirical finding — the paper’s argument is the Political Coase Theorem framing — but they are an interesting set of observations that illuminate the institutional architecture of the 1884 ennoblement, and they cluster cleanly with the paper’s reading of the kazoku system as institutional bargain rather than discretionary reward.
Under the naive sōdaka (草高) reading of the threshold rule, 93 domains in the dataset show “deviations” from the 1884 mechanical prediction. That’s a lot of deviations, and an earlier literature (Lebra 1993, for example) reads many of them as discretionary Boshin punishments and rewards.
Two examples were often cited under that reading:
The deviations-as-discretionary-punishment reading would be one way to support a “Boshin loyalty matters for rank” story. But it falls apart when you re-baseline the threshold to the figure the rule actually used.

text:
仙台藩 (Sendai, Date) — sōdaka ~620,000 koku (well into marquess territory), but received only count. Lebra read this as a Boshin penalty.
会津藩 → 斗南藩 (Aizu → Tonami) — pre-Boshin sōdaka ~230,000 koku, but the post-Boshin remnant received only viscount.

title: 20  Patterns in the 1884 rank assignments
text:
20.2 The Asami (1994) correction
But Asami (1994) argues the threshold rule operated on genmai (現米) — the post-1869 tax-reported rice flow, which is much lower than sōdaka for most domains.
Re-checking:

Sendai genmai = 67,740 koku → squarely in count range. Sendai’s rank is mechanical, not punishment.
斗南 genmai = 7,380 koku → squarely in viscount range. Also mechanical.

(The post-Boshin territorial reduction of Aizu from 23万石 to 3万石 was itself the punishment — at the territorial level, not the rank level.)






NoteA third interpretive frame for Sendai — the Kanpō #307 「依父勳功特授伯爵」 finding (added 2026-05-25)





The project’s transcription of Kanpō 第307号 (primary_source_transcriptions/kazoku_rei_decree_kanpo_307.md) records an additional formal categorization for Sendai-Date that the rank-deviation literature has not emphasized:

從五位　伊達 宗基 ← 依父勳功特授伯爵 (Junior Fifth Rank, Date Munemoto, Count rank specially conferred by the FATHER’S meritorious service)

The 「依父勳功特授」 (“specially conferred by the father’s meritorious service”) prefix puts Date Munemoto on the merit-based ennoblement track — the same legal track as the 83 Restoration-era operational architects (Itō, Yamagata, Inoue, Saigō Tsugumichi, etc.). The Sendai-Date Count rank is not lineage-mechanical in the way other count-tier daimyō ranks were; it’s an inherited merit ennoblement on its father’s recorded service.
This means three different interpretive frames now exist for the Sendai-Date Count rank:








Frame
Source
Reading




Boshin-penalty
Lebra (1993)
Sōdaka ~620k put Sendai in marquess range; the count rank was a punishment for Northern Alliance leadership.


Genmai-mechanical
Asami (1994)
Genmai ~67k put Sendai in count range; the rank was the threshold-rule outcome (no punishment at the rank level).


Patrilineal-merit
Kanpō #307 (2026-05-25 transcription)
The formal category was 「依父勳功特授伯爵」 — Date Munemoto received Count rank as a merit-track ennoblement inherited from his father’s service, neither pure-lineage nor pure-genmai-mechanical.



The three frames are not strictly mutually exclusive: the genmai threshold supplied the rank tier (Asami); the patrilineal-merit formalism supplied the legal track (Kanpō); and the territorial reduction that lowered the genmai in the first place was Sendai’s Boshin penalty (Lebra). All three describe different layers of the same outcome. For paper writing, the safest framing is: “the kazoku-rei did not impose a discretionary Boshin penalty at the rank level for Sendai; the count rank was both the genmai-threshold mechanical outcome (Asami 1994) and a formal patrilineal merit-track ennoblement (Kanpō #307).”



Applying the genmai rule mechanically: of the 93 “kokudaka (石高) rule” deviations, 76 disappear (they were mechanical genmai outcomes all along). Only 19 remain — the truly anomalous cases.

Of 9 Restoration-service upgrades, 8 are anti-bakufu / pro-restoration, 1 is pro-bakufu (Tatsuoka, but the family had Restoration service via 大給松平).
The 3 pro-bakufu deviations in the full table are:

Shizuoka — the Tokugawa main line, governed by the Tokugawa-main-line clause
Mito — gosanke exception clause (the 1884 叙爵内規 had a clause written specifically to elevate Mito above the genmai threshold)
Tatsuoka — Ōgyū-Matsudaira, classified pro-bakufu by family-affiliation but had Restoration service contributions

So the apparent “pro-bakufu also get deviations” pattern is explained by separate rule provisions, not Boshin alignment per se.

title: 20  Patterns in the 1884 rank assignments
section: 20.7 Why this is a clean paper finding
text:
20.7 Why this is a clean paper finding
The simple conditional probability P(deviation | Boshin alignment) is statistically uninformative — roughly equal across groups (6.4% anti-bakufu vs 7.3% pro-bakufu). The naive marginal effect of Boshin alignment on “any deviation” is small.
But the structure of deviations is highly informative:

All 9 Restoration-service upgrades → anti-bakufu side
All 3 pro-bakufu deviations → Tokugawa-cadet exception clauses
All 8 viscount→baron downgrades → former tsukegarō (付家老) or hatamoto (旗本) houses (orthogonal to Boshin)

The paper’s core empirical claim is therefore narrow but strong: the 1884 peerage system rewarded Restoration-service families specifically; the rule-bound mechanism of those rewards is identifiable in the data.

text:
20.9 The 19, one by one
This section walks each of the 19 cases in turn, organised by deviation type. For families already covered in earlier walkthroughs — Shimazu (chapter 02b), Mōri (chapter 02c), and the five viscount→count houses (chapter 02i) — this section gives a paragraph summary with a cross-link. For the remaining 12 cases (徳川家達, 徳川篤敬, 松平茂昭, 伊達宗徳, and all eight viscount→baron houses), this section provides first-pass per-family narratives.






TipReading aid



The same family-not-domain framing from the top of this chapter applies throughout. Where a block says “Mōri received prince,” it means the Mōri family received prince rank in 1884, indexed to the domain (Chōshū) they ruled in 1870. Domains were abolished in 1871.



20.9.1 A. Marquess → Prince (3 cases)
The three prince rank assignments span the full Boshin spectrum: two anti-bakufu Restoration leaders (Shimazu of Satsuma, Mōri of Chōshū) and the Tokugawa main-line exception (Tokugawa Iesato, post-1868 head of the Sunpu/Shizuoka relocation).

20.9.1.1 薩摩藩 — Shimazu, Restoration prize







Family
島津家 (Shimazu)

島津忠義

1884 outcome
prince (公爵)


Boshin role
Anti-bakufu Restoration core


Why prince
Restoration-leader exception — named in the rule provisions



A genmai of nearly 300,000 koku would place Shimazu solidly in marquess territory under the standard rule. The prince rank is the explicit Restoration-leader clause of the 1884 kazoku-rei: Shimazu (Satsuma) and Mōri (Chōshū) are named in the rule provisions as recipients of prince rank for their leadership of the Restoration. (See chapter 02b for the full Shimazu / Satsuma walkthrough.)


20.9.1.2 長州藩 — Mōri, Restoration prize

Boshin role
Anti-bakufu Restoration core; Kiheitai + standing army


Why prince
Restoration-leader exception — same clause as Shimazu



A genmai of 224,000 koku is well into marquess range. The prince rank is the matching half of the named Restoration-leader exception. The Mōri family’s distinctive contribution beyond the joint Sat-Chō diplomacy was the modernised standing army, built around the Kiheitai and related 諸隊. (See chapter 02c for the full Mōri / Chōshū walkthrough.)


20.9.1.3 静岡藩 — Tokugawa main-line exception







Family
徳川家 (Tokugawa main line)


1884 recipient
徳川家達


kokudaka (Hansei Ichiran omotedaka (表高))
699,182 koku (post-1868 relocation; pre-1868 shogunal tenryō was vastly larger)


Genmai

Genmai-rule expectation
marquess


1884 outcome
prince (公爵)


Boshin role
Pro-bakufu (this is the former bakufu)


Why prince
Tokugawa main-line exception — its own clause, distinct from the gosanke and Restoration-leader clauses



After the bakufu’s defeat in 1868 the Tokugawa main line was relocated from Edo to a 70-万-石 territory centred on Sunpu (modern Shizuoka). The defeated party was nevertheless granted prince rank in 1884 because the kazoku-rei contained an explicit Tokugawa-main-line clause — separate from both the sanke clause (covering Owari/Kii/Mito) and the Restoration-leader clause (covering Shimazu/Mōri). 徳川家達 (1863–1940), great-grandson of the 11th shōgun (将軍) Ienari and grandson by adoption to the 14th shōgun Iemochi, was the recipient.
The methodological point: the 1884 rules ennobled by what the rules said, not by who won the war. The defeated central party received the highest rank — because the rules said so.



20.9.2 B. Count → Marquess (3 cases)
Three families received marquess rather than the genmai-predicted count. One was the explicit gosanke clause (Mito-Tokugawa); the other two were Restoration-loyalty rewards arriving years after the initial 1884 ennoblements (1888 for Echizen-Matsudaira/Fukui, 1891 for Date-Uwajima).

20.9.2.1 水戸藩 — Mito-Tokugawa, the gosanke clause







Family
水戸徳川家 (one of the gosanke (御三家))


1884 recipient
徳川篤敬


kokudaka (Hansei Ichiran gōdaka)
306,776 koku


Genmai
58,553 koku


Genmai-rule expectation
count


1884 outcome
marquess (侯爵)


Boshin role
Pro-bakufu by family-affiliation (the family produced the 15th shōgun Yoshinobu; Mito’s internal politics were violently divided)


Why marquess
Gosanke clause — explicit rule provision for the three Tokugawa cadet houses



Mito’s genmai of 58,553 koku sits squarely in count range. The 1884 kazoku-rei nevertheless elevated the family to marquess because of an explicit “徳川旧三家” clause: the heads of the three gosanke (御三家: Owari, Kii, Mito) were granted marquess rank regardless of genmai. Asami (1994), pp. 109–110, reproduces the rule text.
The clause was written for all three gosanke; Owari and Kii’s genmai were high enough to qualify for marquess on the standard rule, so only Mito triggered the actual exception. (Mito’s revenue had been substantially reduced through the 1860s; Owari and Kii were less affected.) Like Shizuoka, this is a case where the deviation is explained by a separately-written rule provision, not by Boshin alignment.


20.9.2.2 福井藩 — Echizen-Matsudaira, 1888 Restoration reward







Family
越前松平家 (Fukui line; founder 結城秀康 was Ieyasu’s second son)


1884 recipient

count (genmai is well within the 50–150k count range)


1884 outcome
count → marquess (侯爵), 1888


Boshin role
Anti-bakufu (joined Restoration coalition through 公議政体派 channels)


Why upgraded
Restoration role — 松平春嶽 (=慶永) was a 四賢侯 / 公議政体派 figure central to the Restoration negotiations



Initially ennobled count in 1884 — exactly the genmai rule’s prediction — the Fukui-Matsudaira family was promoted to marquess in 1888. The citation given was the role of the previous head 松平春嶽 (松平慶永, 1828–1890) in the Meiji Restoration. Shungaku led the public-deliberation reformist faction within the broader Tokugawa orbit, served briefly as the bakufu’s 政事総裁職 in 1862, brokered between bakufu and court during the 1864–1867 negotiations, and joined the new government’s senior advisory ranks in 1868–1872.
The 1888 timing matters: it shows the 1884 cohort was not the only ennoblement event. The Meiji peerage system allowed promotion for Restoration service on a rolling basis as historical assessments matured. Fukui is the canonical example of this rolling-promotion mechanism.


20.9.2.3 宇和島藩 — Date-Uwajima, 1891 Restoration reward







Family
伊予宇和島伊達家 (Sendai-Date cadet line)

1884 outcome
count → marquess (侯爵), 1891


Boshin role
Anti-bakufu — 伊達宗城 (Munenari) was a 四賢侯 figure who joined the Restoration faction


Why upgraded
Restoration role — Munenari’s status as a 公議政体派 figure and Restoration-government advisor



Uwajima received count rank in 1884 — exactly what the genmai rule predicts. In 1891 the family was promoted to marquess, citing the role of 伊達宗城 (1818–1892), one of the 四賢侯 (Four Wise Lords) of late-Tokugawa Japan alongside Shungaku of Fukui, 山内容堂 of Tosa, and 島津斉彬 of Satsuma. Like Shungaku, Munenari was a bridging figure during the 1860s court-bakufu negotiations and served in senior advisory roles in the early Meiji government.
The structural irony: the main Date line at Sendai received only count rank (because their post-Boshin genmai of 67,740 koku fell into the count band — itself partly a Boshin-induced territorial reduction), while the Date cadet at Uwajima was promoted past them to marquess. The Date name appears on both sides of the Restoration ledger.



20.9.3 C. Viscount → Count (5 cases — see chapter 02i for full narratives)
The five viscount→count upgrades are the cleanest empirical evidence for the paper’s central claim: families with genmai well below 50,000 koku who received count rank anyway, 5 of 5 on the Restoration-aligned side (counting Tatsuoka’s personal post-1868 service for 大給恒).

20.9.4 D. Viscount → Baron (8 cases — post-1868 newly-elevated daimyō)
All eight viscount→baron downgrades share a single structural feature: each family became “true daimyō” only at the end of the Tokugawa era or at the Restoration itself. Either they were 付家老 (vassals-of-a-vassal, attached to a major-domain house) detached at the 1868 reorganisation, or they were 旗本 (direct Tokugawa banner-men) bumped above the 10,000-koku daimyō threshold in 1868–1869.
The 1884 kazoku-rei contained a separate provision for these post-1868 elevations: rather than receiving viscount as the genmai rule would predict, they were placed at baron, reflecting their pre-Restoration baishin / hatamoto status. The downgrade is therefore explained by pre-1868 institutional history, not by Boshin alignment — seven of the eight are coded anti-bakufu and one is neutral (Nariwa); none is pro-bakufu. The pattern is orthogonal to Restoration coalition.
The eight split 4 + 4 between the two routes.
The 4 付家老 (vassal-of-a-vassal) cases:

20.9.4.1 今尾藩 — 竹腰家, Owari 付家老







Family
竹腰家 (Takenokoshi)

Boshin role
Anti-bakufu (followed Owari-Tokugawa’s late-Restoration alignment)


Why baron
付家老 — Owari attached house, only independent post-1868



The Takenokoshi were one of three 付家老 houses attached to Owari-Tokugawa (along with Ishikawa and Naruse). Their function for centuries was to serve as senior advisors and de-facto overseers of the Owari main line on the bakufu’s behalf — a structural device by which the bakufu kept direct visibility into the major cadet branches. The Takenokoshi held about 30,000 koku in Mino (around Imao, modern Gifu) but counted as Owari baishin rather than independent daimyō. In 1868 the 付家老 system was dissolved and the three Owari attached houses became formally independent. The 1884 baron rank reflects this very recent independence.

Of the Kii (紀州) attached structure, two 付家老 houses — Mizuno of 新宮 (eastern Kii) and Andō of 田辺 (western Kii) — were detached at the Restoration. The Mizuno had administered eastern Kii from Shingū for over two centuries while formally remaining Kii baishin. Their post-1868 independence + baron rank follows the same pattern as the Owari 付家老.

The Nakayama were Mito’s 付家老 — the structural parallel to Takenokoshi in Owari. Their seat at Matsuoka (modern Ibaraki) was small, but they had long held senior administrative roles within the Mito han, including arbiter functions during Mito’s violently divided 1864–1868 internal politics (the 天狗党 / 諸生党 conflict). The last bakumatsu-era family head was 安藤直裕 (1827–1909). Post-1868 detachment + baron rank pattern as in the other 付家老 cases.
The 4 旗本 → 1万石 daimyō-elevation cases:

The Hondō were a Hitachi (modern Ibaraki) 旗本 (hatamoto) house at 8,000 koku for most of the Tokugawa era — just below the 10,000-koku daimyō threshold the bakufu used to distinguish daimyō from banner-men. In 1868 the new imperial government bumped them to 10,000 koku, making them nominally daimyō. Their 1884 baron rank reflects this very recent elevation; they had never been “real” multigenerational daimyō of the kind the standard viscount-tier rule envisioned.


20.9.4.6 成羽藩 — 山崎家, 交代寄合 elevation







Family

The Yamasaki of Nariwa (modern Okayama, 高梁市) were 交代寄合 (kōtai yoriai) — an intermediate status between 旗本 and full daimyō that gave the family some daimyō-like privileges (sankin kōtai attendance) without being formally counted as daimyō. They held about 5,000 koku for most of the Tokugawa era and were promoted to 10,000 koku daimyō status in 1868. Their baron rank follows the same post-1868 elevation rule.
Nariwa is the lone “neutral” in this 8-case group — neither anti- nor pro-bakufu in the Boshin classification. This is consistent with the deeper claim that the viscount→baron downgrades are about pre-Restoration institutional status rather than Boshin alignment.

The Hirano of Tawaramoto (modern Nara, 磯城郡) were a long-standing 旗本 house at 5,000 koku elevated to 10,000 koku in 1868. Same post-1868-elevation pattern as the other three 旗本/交代寄合 cases.


20.9.4.8 矢島藩 — 生駒家, late 旗本 elevation with active Boshin role







Family
生駒家 (Ikoma)


1884 recipient
生駒親敬


kokudaka (Hansei Ichiran sōdaka)
16,331 koku


Genmai
6,228 koku


Genmai-rule expectation
viscount


1884 outcome

Boshin role
Anti-bakufu — Northern Alliance member who defected to the imperial side during the war


Why baron
旗本-equivalent → 1万石 elevation only in 1869



The most institutionally interesting case in this 8-group. The Ikoma had been daimyō of Sanuki-Takamatsu (讃岐高松藩, ~17 万石) until the 1640 御家騒動 (succession scandal), after which the main domain was confiscated and the family reduced to a small Dewa (modern Akita) fief at 8,000 koku — 旗本-equivalent status. They held this position for over two centuries.
In the Boshin War (Boshin sensō) the Ikoma joined the Northern Alliance (奥羽越列藩同盟) but defected to the imperial side mid-war — one of the Northern Alliance defectors covered in chapter 02g. The new government rewarded the defection by elevating them back to 10,000-koku daimyō status in 1869. Despite this active anti-bakufu service, however, the 1884 kazoku-rei still placed them at baron under the post-1868-elevation provision. The Ikoma case is therefore especially diagnostic: it shows the baron downgrade was structural (about pre-Restoration daimyō status) rather than discretionary (Boshin reward or punishment).

Methodological hook: 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治 (Kazoku tanjō: meiyo to taimen no Meiji, “Birth of the Peerage: Honor and Reputation in Meiji”)』リブロポート (1994), pp. 87–88 explicitly articulates the genmai threshold rule. Lebra (1993) uses the kokudaka framing. The dataset stores both deviation values side by side — the kokudaka-based (Lebra) variant and the genmai-based (Asami) variant — so the paper can present whichever baseline the analysis calls for.

title: 21  Sources and citations
text:
21.1 The three source families
This chapter documents the citation chain behind every variable in the analytic dataset: what each source is, what variables it backs, and the form in which it should be cited.
The dataset draws on three structurally different families of sources, each with its own role and citation pattern.

title: 21  Sources and citations
text:
21.1.1 Family 1 — Hansei Ichiran 1870 (the primary record)
The book itself: 藩制一覧 (Hansei Ichiran), 1870, two volumes, surveyed and compiled by the early Meiji government. Every hi_* column in the analytic dataset derives from this source, traced through per-domain extracted chunks.
Citation form: Hansei Ichiran (藩制一覧), 1870, vol. N, table N, page N. Compiled by the Meiji-government survey of daimyō* (大名) administrative returns.*
The exact page reference for any Hansei Ichiran-derived value is preserved in the dataset’s master extraction record (one row per per-domain per-field extraction, with the source crop / page noted). When a paper table cites a specific Hansei Ichiran number, this row-level audit trail establishes precisely where in the original document the value lives.
Volume contents:

Volume 1: each domain’s fiscal and demographic data — kokudaka (石高) in its several variants, tax receipts, population, household counts.
Volume 2: each domain’s military and administrative data — troop strength, warships, officer rosters. Saga (佐嘉藩) is documented as a gap in Volume 2’s military rosters; the dataset substitutes 藤野保 (2010) as a published scholarly substitute for Saga’s military information.



21.1.2 Family 2 — Government primary documents (paper-citable)
Three contemporaneous government compilations that overlap with Hansei Ichiran on population and revenue, plus the official ennoblement gazettes:

21.1.2.1 明治史要 (Meiji Shiyō)
Compiled by the 修史局 (Shūshikyoku, “Bureau of Historiography”). The standard early-Meiji compendium of administrative data: domain populations, household counts, kokudaka, basic fiscal indicators. Backs the population_meiji_shiyo and households_meiji_shiyo columns.
Citation form: 明治史要 (Meiji Shiyō), Bureau of Historiography (修史局), early Meiji compendium.


21.1.2.2 大隈重信関係資料 (Ōkuma Shigenobu kankei shiryō, “Ōkuma Shigenobu Papers”)「府藩県石高人口表 (Fu-han-ken kokudaka jinkō-hyō, “Prefecture-Domain-County Kokudaka and Population Table”)」
Held in the Ōkuma Shigenobu papers (大隈重信関係資料) at the Waseda University Library, call number イ14-A4361. The “府藩県石高人口表” is a contemporary tabulation of administrative-unit kokudaka and population. Backs the population_okuma and uchidaka_okuma_koku columns.
📄 Download the scanned manuscript (1.7 MB PDF) — Waseda University Library digital archive, freely accessible.
Citation form: 「府藩県石高人口表」, 大隈重信関係資料, Ōkuma Shigenobu papers, Waseda University Library archival collection (call number イ14-A4361).


21.1.2.3 旧高旧領取調帳 (Kyūdaka kyūryō torishirabechō, “Register of Former Kokudaka and Former Fief-Holders”), 1872 Meiji cadastral survey
The early Meiji government’s per-village cadastral survey, compiled 1872 from the village-level land-yield rolls (kokudaka) that the daimyō domains had been administering pre-haihan-chiken. The “旧” (“former”) in the title refers to the pre-1869 Edo-period feudal arrangement: each village entry records (a) its old province (旧国), district (旧郡), village (旧村), and lord (旧領); (b) its rated kokudaka (旧高); and (c) the 1872 Meiji prefecture (旧県) administering it post-haihan.
Hosted as a free open-access searchable database by the National Museum of Japanese History (国立歴史民俗博物館 / Rekihaku):
📄 旧高旧領取調帳データベース (Rekihaku) — freely accessible; query by 旧領名 / 旧国 / 旧郡 / 旧村.
Backs the rekihaku_kyudaka_iv_koku column, which feeds the composite kokudaka_iv as Tier 4 for the 13 domains absent from HI vol 1’s kokudaka coverage (仙台, 新発田, 杵築, 母里, 清崎, 足利, 須坂, 岩村, 対馬府中, 岩国, 岩崎, 松岡, 斗南). The Rekihaku 旧高 is a kusadaka-tier metric (rated land yield from village rolls) — the same conceptual tier as HI 草高 / 合高 — restoring methodological consistency to the IV across all 286 rows. See CORRECTIONS_LOG.md Section K for the full audit.
For 仙台藩 specifically the IV uses only the post-Boshin retained territory (仙台県 + 宮城県 + distant 常陸/下総 hatamoto enclaves) = 299,659 koku, EXCLUDING the four 1869 carve-off prefectures (登米県 + 胆沢県 + 江刺県 + 角田県 = 674,350 koku) that were no longer Sendai-Date revenue at the moment of haihan-chiken.
Citation form: 旧高旧領取調帳 (1872 Meiji cadastral survey), per 国立歴史民俗博物館 旧高旧領取調帳データベース (https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param), accessed 2026-05-24.


21.1.2.4 統計集誌 (Tōkei Shūshi, “Statistical Journal”) “人員表 (Jin’in-hyō,”Personnel Table”)”
Compiled by 呉文聡 from 野村文夫’s materials, published in Tōkei Shūshi (the early-Meiji statistical journal). Backs the population_tokei_shushi column.
Citation form: 統計集誌 (Tōkei Shūshi), 人員表, compiled by 呉文聡 from 野村文夫 materials.


21.1.2.5 官報 (Kanpō, “Official Gazette”) 第307号 (1884-07-08) — primary ennoblement gazette (daimyō / sekke (摂家) cohort)
The government gazette that recorded the 1884-07-07 ennoblement decree — the original 公侯伯子男 (prince / marquess / count / viscount / baron) rank assignments. This is the primary record underlying every kazoku_rank_actual value, with a small number of late-cohort exceptions handled by Kanpō #308.
📄 View the gazette (NDL Digital Collections, pid 2943511, 9 pages) — freely accessible, Public Domain Mark.
Citation form: 官報 第307号 (1884-07-08), NDL pid 2943511.


21.1.2.6 官報 第308号 (1884-07-09) — primary ennoblement gazette (late cohort)
The follow-up gazette for daimyō whose rank assignments lagged the main decree by one day. Backs a small number of kazoku_rank_actual rows including 半原藩 and several other edge cases.
📄 View the gazette (NDL Digital Collections, pid 2943512, 9 pages) — freely accessible, Public Domain Mark.
Citation form: 官報 第308号 (1884-07-09), NDL Digital Collections (pid 2943512).



21.1.3 Family 3 — Scholarly synthesis (paper-citable)
Secondary scholarly works treated not as the original record but as the synthesis-and-interpretation layer between the primary documents and the dataset’s coded values.

21.1.3.1 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治 (Kazoku tanjō: meiyo to taimen no Meiji, “Birth of the Peerage: Honor and Reputation in Meiji”)』(リブロポート 1994)
The single most-important scholarly source for the dataset’s central kazoku (華族)-rank variables. Asami’s work:

Reconstructs the 1884 kazoku-rei threshold rule with sufficient detail that the genmai (現米)-based mechanical-expected rank (kazoku_rank_expected_genmai) can be computed for every domain.
Establishes genmai — not sōdaka (草高) — as the threshold numerator. Pre-Asami sources (including Lebra 1993) implicitly used kokudaka as the threshold; Asami shows the rule explicitly operated on post-1869 genmai. This single point reduces the apparent “Boshin penalty” sample from 93 cases (Lebra framing) to 19 (Asami framing) by mechanically explaining 76 outcomes. The Sendai and Aizu cases that look like Restoration punishments turn out to be genmai-mechanical (see chapter 07).
Documents the explicit exception clauses (gosanke clause, Tokugawa-main-line clause, Restoration-leader clause, sankyō (御三卿) clause, post-1868-elevation clause) — each of which explains a specific subset of deviations from the standard rule.

Pages 87–88 articulate the genmai rule; pages 109–110 give the gosanke and sankyō clauses; pages 105–115 the Restoration-leader exception. Asami is the citation in 94 percent of kazoku_rank_source rows.
Citation form: 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治』リブロポート (1994).


21.1.3.2 Beasley, W.G. The Meiji Restoration. Stanford University Press (1972)
The standard English-language synthesis of the Restoration period. Used for:

Domain classifications — Beasley p. xii lists “Principal Domains in 1860” with explicit shinpan (親藩) / fudai (譜代) / tozama designations; this is the cross-check used to confirm 仙台 (Date) and 対馬府中 (Sō) as tozama (外様).
Boshin alignment — Beasley ch. XI pp. 295–299 provides the standard chronology of Toba-Fushimi and the subsequent imperial consolidation. The bulk boshin_alignment_source entries for roughly 212 small viscount-tier domains cite Beasley.
Hikone defection — Beasley p. 297 is the only English-language source that explicitly names Hikone as the pre-Toba-Fushimi defector (the Ii family broke with the bakufu (幕府) after Ii Naosuke’s assassination in 1860 and the subsequent Sakuradamon Incident).

Citation form: Beasley, W. G. The Meiji Restoration. Stanford University Press (1972).


21.1.3.3 Esposito, Gabriele. Japanese Armies 1868–1877: The Boshin War and Satsuma Rebellion. Osprey Men-at-Arms 530 (2020)
A military-history reference with detailed coverage of the Boshin War (戊辰戦争) combatants. Used for Boshin alignment citations on the major-domain combatants (the focal four plus the Northern Alliance core: Aizu / 斗南, Sendai, Yonezawa, Shōnai, Nagaoka, Nihonmatsu, Morioka, Kuwana). Backs ten boshin_alignment_source rows.
The volume is in the project workspace. Page guide for Boshin-alignment claims:

pp. 5–7 — Conflicts with Chōshū 1863–66 + European military missions
pp. 8–11 — Boshin War narrative: Operations January–July 1868 (Toba-Fushimi, Sat-Chō-Tosa imperial alliance), Northern Alliance May–October 1868, Republic of Ezo October 1868–June 1869
p. 22 — South-western forces (Satsuma + Chōshū modernisation)
pp. 23–28 — Shogunate forces, Denshutai, police, Aizu forces, Hokkaido 1869
pp. 32–33 — Chōshū forces (Kiheitai etc.)

Citation form: Esposito, Gabriele. Japanese Armies 1868–1877: The Boshin War and Satsuma Rebellion. Osprey Men-at-Arms 530, Osprey Publishing (2020), specific pages as cited.


21.1.3.4 工藤威『奥羽列藩同盟の基礎的研究 (Ōu reppan dōmei no kisoteki kenkyū, “Basic Research on the Northern Alliance”)』(岩田書院 2002)
The standard Japanese-language academic monograph on the 奥羽越列藩同盟 (Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei) — the Northern Alliance of bakumatsu domains that resisted the imperial advance after Toba-Fushimi. Backs the Boshin alignment citations for the twelve Northern Alliance combatants plus the nine Northern Alliance defectors who switched sides mid-war, and supplied the corrections to several Northern Alliance roster mis-classifications.
The monograph is not in the project workspace and would need to be acquired via interlibrary loan. The roster (the load-bearing content for this dataset) is transcribed in the JP Wikipedia article on 奥羽越列藩同盟, which footnotes back to Kudō’s specific pages — the dataset cites Kudō via the Wikipedia transcription with that as the verification chain.
Citation form: 工藤威『奥羽列藩同盟の基礎的研究』岩田書院 (2002), roster transcribed in JP Wikipedia 奥羽越列藩同盟.


21.1.3.5 児玉幸多 ed. 『藩史総覧 (Hanshi sōran, “Comprehensive Survey of Han History”)』新人物往来社 (1977)
The canonical Japanese-language han-classification compendium. The principal source for domain_category (shinpan / fudai / tozama / sankyō / tenryō (天領)). Establishes formal category designations for all roughly 300 domains.
Citation form: 児玉幸多 編『藩史総覧』新人物往来社 (1977).


21.1.3.6 国史大辞典 (Kokushi daijiten, “Great Dictionary of National History”) (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1979–1997)
The standard Japanese-language reference dictionary. Used as the secondary verification layer for individual house histories, daimyō biographies, and domain administrative changes. Not typically cited per row, but listed in the paper bibliography as the underlying reference framework.
Citation form: 国史大辞典 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1979–1997 edition).


21.1.3.7 華族大鑑 (Kazoku taikan, “Compendium of the Peerage”)刊行会 編『華族大鑑』(1990)
The dedicated reference for the 1884 prince-rank (公爵) ennoblements specifically — used for the three prince cases in the dataset (Shimazu / Satsuma, Mōri / Chōshū, Tokugawa / Shizuoka) and as a supplementary citation alongside Asami for several marquess cases.
Citation form: 華族大鑑刊行会 編『華族大鑑』(1990).


21.1.3.8 小田部雄次『華族 近代日本貴族の虚像と実像 (Kazoku: kindai Nihon kizoku no kyozō to jitsuzō, “The Peerage: Image and Reality of the Modern Japanese Aristocracy”)』中公新書 (2006)
A modern general-reader-facing book on the kazoku system as a whole. Used for several small viscount-tier kazoku_rank_source rows where the family didn’t appear in Asami’s main tables.
Citation form: 小田部雄次『華族 近代日本貴族の虚像と実像』中公新書 (2006).


21.1.3.9 松田敬之『〈華族爵位〉請願人名辞典 (Kazoku shakui seigan jinmei jiten, “Dictionary of Peerage-Title Petitioners”)』吉川弘文館 (2015)
Definitive on the 1884 exclusions — daimyō families that were not ennobled because of the kaieki (改易, confiscation) or similar disqualifying events. Backs the documented exclusion of 堀江藩 (大沢家, 万石事件 1871) and the verification of 牟原藩 (resolved as an OCR misreading of 半原藩).
Citation form: 松田敬之『〈華族爵位〉請願人名辞典』吉川弘文館 (2015).


21.1.3.10 Mark Ravina. Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan. Stanford University Press (1999)
Provides the dataset’s ratio variables (kusadaka/commoner, retainers/100 commoners, gendaka (現高)/commoner, retainers/1000 koku, gendaka/kusadaka ratio) from Appendix Table A1 of the book. 227 domains in the dataset have Ravina coverage. One confirmed typo: Ravina A1’s value for Akita 久保田藩 gendaka_per_commoner is 0.049 but the primary-derived value is 0.49 (a decimal-place error). The dataset uses 0.49 (the corrected Ravina value) or the primary-derived 0.457, and flags the discrepancy explicitly.
Citation form: Mark Ravina. Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan. Stanford University Press (1999), Appendix Table A1.


21.1.3.11 Focal-domain published-source supplements
For each of the four focal domains (Saga, Satsuma, Chōshū, Tosa) a published-source supplement lists citable references for military, political, and institutional details that go beyond what Hansei Ichiran provides:

Saga supplement — 藤野保『佐賀藩』(2010); Jentschura 1977 (warships); Beasley 1972
Satsuma supplement — Esposito 2020; Jentschura 1977; Beasley 1972; Craig 1961/2000
Chōshū supplement — Craig 1961/2000; Jansen 1961/1994
Tosa supplement — Roberts 1998; Jansen 1961/1994

Each supplement provides a citation-ready reference block for that focal domain’s narrative section.

The boshin_alignment_source column has a hierarchical citation pattern reflecting the difficulty of citing a single source for all 286 domains:

The bulk-citation pattern (the 212 anti-bakufu, 20 Tokugawa-affiliate, and 10 neutral domains) uses a single Beasley-1972-chapter-XI reference that frames the standard chronology rather than enumerating each domain individually. Beasley’s analysis is that after Toba-Fushimi the imperial side issued a general directive and most small domains complied without taking an active combatant role. The dataset follows this — coding them anti-bakufu by end-state rather than by Boshin participation.

title: 21  Sources and citations
section: 21.5 Materials used as finders only — never cited
text:
21.5 Materials used as finders only — never cited
Two ancillary materials are retained for internal validation but never appear as citations.

21.5.1 Japanese Wikipedia (and English Wikipedia)
Used as a finder to:

Locate primary-source candidates (when a Wikipedia article cites Meiji Shiyō or 浅見 1994, those underlying sources get verified and cited).
Cross-validate values that already have a citable source (when Asami 1994 says marquess and the Japanese Wikipedia article on the family also says marquess, that confirms transcription).
Seed initial values for last_lord and provincial-location fields. Those fields are kept only where they have an independent primary citation.

Wikipedia is never the citation; the underlying primary source it references is.


21.5.2 Bakumatsu-era domain-listing website
Used as an internal cross-validation layer for:

domain_category initial coding (now fully superseded by 児玉 1977 + Beasley 1972 re-citation).
boshin_alignment_group initial coding (now fully superseded by the per-row citation chain documented above).
last_lord and provincial fields (retained as cross-checks; would require independent re-citation before any paper inclusion).

This source is never cited. It was retained only because the initial bulk-data scrape was the spine that allowed the dataset to bootstrap. Every paper-relevant field has since been re-cited to an academic source.

title: 22  Data quality and the audit framework
text:
22.1 The four audit layers
Any analytic result built on analytic_wide.csv is only as defensible as the values it ingests. This chapter walks the four-layer audit framework used to build confidence in the dataset, the cumulative reduction in known issues, the high-impact fixes that landed, and what remains uncertain — documented openly rather than hidden.
crumbs: 22  Data quality and the audit framework

title: 22  Data quality and the audit framework
text:
22.1.1 Layer 1 — Within-domain ratio sanity
For each domain the audit computes ratios that ought to make demographic sense and flags any that don’t:








Ratio
Plausible range
Flag trigger




Average household size (人口 / 戸數)
3–7 persons
Outside 3–7


shizoku (士族) persons per household
3–8 persons
Outside 3–8


sotsu (卒族) persons per household
3–8 persons
Outside 3–8


Population per koku
0.5–5 persons / koku
Outside 0.5–5


Status-class share of total population
Each less than 50%
Implausible class shares



When a flag fires, the audit identifies a specific root cause: OCR misread, mislabelling (sub-total mistaken for total), cross-page contamination, or genuine demographic anomaly.
Notable resolutions from this audit:








Domain
Issue
Resolution




一宮藩
人口 = 3,201 (impossibly far below the population-per-koku floor)
OCR misread of 14,240; corrected via crop re-read


加納藩
戸數 = 317 reported as the domain total
Was actually the 城下町 (castle-town) subset; relabelled


亀田藩
shizoku population = 23,258
Subtotal mislabelled; correct domain-wide total of 23,894 added


成羽藩
人口 = 494
Was 人員(寺領分) — temple-land subset; relabelled


吉見藩
人口 = 372
Was 人員 士卒族 — combined sub-class subset; relabelled


仙台藩
A “兵卒 18,913 persons” row that contained 5,171 women
Was the sotsu (sotsu-class) household population, not active troops; relabelled. Active troops corrected to 14,262.


前橋藩
shizoku household count equal to shizoku person count (4,656)
Alias-rule regression — the bare “shizoku” prefix matched “shizoku (人口)”; alias rule corrected



The audit started at 37 flags and dropped to 26 after these fixes plus the contamination resolutions below. The remaining 26 flags are documented as definitional (for example 人吉藩’s high sotsu ratio is real: Hitoyoshi-Sagara had a vast sotsu-class peasant-soldier population, confirmed against Meiji Shiyō).


22.1.2 Layer 2 — Cross-page contamination audits
Hansei Ichiran is a printed book; many of its pages contain two domains stacked vertically (upper and lower halves of a page). The extraction pipeline assigns rows to one canonical domain per page based on the visible 藩 header. When a page has two domains, the assignment can drift — values from the lower domain accidentally tagged as the upper, or vice versa.

22.1.2.1 2a — Exact-match audit
The exact-match audit identifies pages containing two or more domains, then checks whether numeric values match exactly across the suspect pairs. 261 PDF pages have two or more domains. The audit found 22 value-collisions; 4 were confirmed contamination and fixed:








Page
Pre-fix issue
Resolution




upper-p87 和歌山 / 金澤
11 Wakayama rows duplicated Kanazawa data (Wakayama’s sōdaka (草高) wrongly showed 1,353,352 — Kanazawa’s value)
Removed; Wakayama now correctly shows sōdaka = 539,469 (true Kii kokudaka (石高))


upper-p151 津山 / 津和野
6 Tsuyama rows were a mix of Tsu (人口 247k) and Tsuwano (sōdaka 75k)
All 6 removed; Tsuyama’s true sōdaka = 104,576 was already on p150 (unaffected)


lower-p60 岸和田 / 菊間
7 Kishiwada rows duplicated Kikuma; Kishiwada’s actual data on p58 had never been extracted
7 wrong rows removed; 12 verified Kishiwada rows added from the correct crop


upper-p81 大溝 / 岡田
岡田藩’s 人口 = 11,433 was actually Ōmizo’s value; Okada’s real population (16,231) had never been extracted
1 wrong row removed; 6 verified Okada rows added from the correct crop





22.1.2.2 2b — Fuzzy near-match audit
A complementary methodology with 0.5 percent relative tolerance — catches contamination where the values aren’t exactly equal but are very close (for example two adjacent 10,000-koku-tier domains reporting similar values). Found 8 candidates; all 8 verified as benign:

5 cases: sequential book page numbers showing up as similar values across nearby pages — incidental, not data contamination.
1 case: small-number coincidence (both values in the 100–200 range).
1 case: Tateyama vs Tawaramoto (both 10,000-koku domains) — confirmed as legitimate independent values that happen to be coincidentally close.
1 case: Himeji vs Hikone sōdaka (草高) tally of 250,000 was initially classified as a benign coincidence (both very large fudai sub-territory tallies) but the 2026-05-27 audit re-investigated this finding and confirmed it as a real transcription error: Himeji’s MASTER raw read 二十五萬石 元高 (= 250,000), but the actual page printed 拾五萬石 (= 150,000). The internal-arithmetic consistency check is dispositive — Himeji’s same-block 合 高 of 212,124 cannot be produced by 250,000 + any positive 外 高. Corrected 2026-05-27.



22.1.2.3 2c — The Tosa-Satsuma column-stack
The most-historically-instructive contamination wasn’t caught by either intra-Hansei Ichiran audit because it involves an external primary source. Tosa’s hi_jinkou value of 896,808 is essentially identical to Satsuma’s population_tokei_shushi value of 896,817 — a column-stack OCR error in the Tōkei Shūshi tabulation pipeline. The dataset substitutes Meiji Shiyō’s 516,867 for Tosa’s population.
A cross-source contamination audit (Hansei Ichiran versus primary-source) would catch this pattern systematically; the current dataset handles the Tosa case as a documented one-off correction.


22.1.2.4 2d — The 2026-05-27 systematic audit
A complete cross-domain value-collision audit was run in May 2026 across all 286 domains × all numeric Hansei Ichiran columns. The audit started with 1,250 raw cross-domain matches; after filtering out coincidences shared by more than two domains (the small-bakumatsu kokudaka coincidences at 10,000 / 12,000 / 27,000 / etc.) the candidate set was 57; after individual crop verification of all 26 unique cells the result was:

1 real transcription error: 姫路藩 sōdaka — see section 2b above for detail. Fixed.
25 confirmed coincidences: small-domain populations or household counts that numerically coincide with another domain’s kokudaka — cross-metric matches that are statistically expected at the magnitudes involved.

The same audit run also resolved the three open cross-page contamination cases (upper-p151 津山/津和野, lower-p60 岸和田/菊間, upper-p81 大溝/岡田) that had been flagged but not closed in the earlier 2026-05-19 audit. Direct crop inspection confirmed all three are clean in the current MASTER.
A separate impossible-state cell investigation caught four transcription errors in 森藩 that had been producing an impossible 0.99 persons/household ratio:

All four were verified by re-reading the high-resolution Hansei Ichiran vol 2 page (PDF p113-L). Post-correction the persons/household ratio resolves to 4.74 (normal).
HI-vs-primary discrepancy investigations of 4 cases where Hansei Ichiran differed from Meiji Shiyō by more than 2× confirmed all 4 are either primary-source-side errors (廣島’s 9,536 vs HI’s 97,536 — primary missing 萬 prefix) or unit-definition differences (泉’s 名 vs 人). HI is correct in all 4.
The audit also caught 3 build-script bugs that had been silently producing wrong values for 庄内 / 湯長谷 / 新庄 (the 人員 (status_class) prefix-match pollution of hi_jinkou), 鶴田 (military-row pollution of hi_shizoku_jinin), and 長州 / 郡山 (missing 内 X 人口 whitelist pattern). All fixed.



22.1.3 Layer 3 — Ravina replication audit
Mark Ravina’s Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan (1999) Appendix Table A1 provides per-domain ratios (kusadaka / commoner, retainers per 100 commoners, gendaka (現高) / commoner, retainers per 1,000 koku, gendaka / kusadaka) for roughly 227 domains. The dataset computes the same ratios both from Hansei Ichiran directly and from primary sources (Ōkuma + Meiji Shiyō + Tōkei Shūshi), and audits the deltas against Ravina.
State after final pass (all 227 Ravina rows merged):

The primary-derived ratios match Ravina substantially better than the Hansei Ichiran-derived ratios (173 clean matches against 78). This reflects that Ravina himself worked from the same primary sources the dataset now cites — Meiji Shiyō and the Ōkuma papers — rather than from Hansei Ichiran directly. The 12 primary-derived divergent cases were individually diagnosed. The notable finding: Akita’s gendaka_per_commoner = 0.049 in Ravina is a confirmed decimal-place typo (should be 0.49 per the primary-derived value).


22.1.4 Layer 4 — The honest-medium tracker
The tracker records every value flagged at medium confidence — explicitly not claiming high-confidence accuracy, with a reason category and reason detail per row.
Starting at 141 medium-confidence rows, the tracker was reduced over a series of cleanup passes to 22 rows. The remaining 22 are structural source-level limits — no further crop re-reading or OCR work will resolve them. These cells affect tail-end fiscal precision and hyper-specific demographic sub-counts. None of them feed into the analytic dataset’s independent variable (kokudaka), dependent variable (kazoku rank), or any control variable.

Sub-koku precision: 七日市藩 内米 = 3,718.04245 koku — the integer 3,718 is confirmed; the .04245 vs .04235 fraction hinges on one ambiguous character. Doesn’t affect any analysis using koku-level precision.

Dense-column ambiguity: 山崎藩 雑税金 = 27.14086 — multiple gold sub-lines on the page; column attribution is not isolable.
Needs higher resolution: 厳原藩 寺院 = 199 — the page may actually read 287 (including 盲僧, blind monks); the scope-distinguishing annotation isn’t crisply readable.
Source illegible: 竜野藩 穢多 = 6 — the label is visible but the number is too faded to verify.

title: 22  Data quality and the audit framework
text:
22.2 High-impact specific fixes
Beyond the audit-driven reductions, several specific fixes had material analytic impact and are worth flagging explicitly because they corrected values that would otherwise have entered analytic tables.

22.2.1 Sendai mislabel — “兵卒 18,913” was actually sotsu demographics
The single highest-impact fix. Sendai’s hi_total_troops_persons field had been populated with 18,913, but the underlying source row labelled “兵卒 (alt)” contained 5,171 women — an impossible mix for an active military unit. Re-examination showed the row was actually Sendai’s sotsu (sotsu-class) household population, not troops.
Corrected to:

hi_total_troops_persons = 14,262 (the correct active military personnel from the 兵士 員 row)
hi_sotsu_jinin = 18,913 (correctly bucketed as demographics)

A latent-mislabel audit was then extended across all military-bucketed rows; no other mislabels of similar magnitude were found.


22.2.2 Tosa 人口 = 896,808 — column-stack OCR error from Satsuma
Tosa’s Hansei Ichiran population value of 896,808 is an OCR column-stack error — it is identical (within 9 persons) to Satsuma’s primary-source population, indicating the tabulation pipeline mis-aligned a column. The dataset substitutes Meiji Shiyō’s 516,867 for Tosa’s population.


22.2.3 Saga — absent from Hansei Ichiran’s military rosters
Saga (佐嘉藩) has a Volume 1 fiscal entry and a partial Volume 2 fiscal entry, but is completely absent from Volume 2’s military rosters — the standard tables of troop strength, officer rosters, and warships that every other major domain has. This is a source-level gap, not an extraction failure. The pipeline handles this via SOURCE_GAP marker rows; Saga’s military details come from a published-source supplement (藤野保 2010, Beasley 1972 — see the Saga walkthrough).


22.2.4 Stacked-page contamination — four confirmed cases
The four cross-page contamination fixes (Wakayama / Kanazawa, Tsuyama / Tsuwano, Kishiwada / Kikuma, Okada / Ōmizo) corrected 11 + 6 + 7 + 1 = 25 wrongly-attributed rows and added 12 + 6 = 18 newly-extracted verified rows. Cumulative impact on the analytic dataset: Wakayama’s kokudaka_iv went from a contaminated 1.35 million (Kanazawa’s value) to its true 539,469; Okada’s hi_jinkou went from 11,433 (Ōmizo’s value) to its true 16,231.


22.2.5 Akita Ravina A1 typo confirmed
Ravina A1’s value for Akita 久保田藩 gendaka_per_commoner is 0.049, which is implausibly low. Primary-derived computation gives 0.457. The dataset uses either 0.49 (the corrected Ravina value, treating the printed 0.049 as a decimal-place typo) or the primary-derived 0.457, and notes the discrepancy explicitly.


22.2.6 Four domain-category reclassifications
Surfaced during the re-sourcing pass that replaced the bakumatsu-website seed values with academic citations:

仙台藩 (Date): originally coded shinpan (親藩) → corrected to tozama per Beasley p. xii and Kokushi daijiten
伊予吉田藩 (Date cadet): shinpan → tozama
対馬府中藩 (Sō): shinpan → tozama (kokushukaku (国主格) status but tozama (外様) by family origin)
桑名藩 (Hisamatsu-Matsudaira): fudai (譜代) → shinpan (Hisamatsu-Matsudaira consistency with 伊予松山 and 今治)

Discussed in detail in the methodology note in chapter 03c.


22.2.7 Five Boshin alignment reclassifications
Also from the re-sourcing pass:

守山藩, 館藩 (= 松前): originally coded pro-bakufu (幕府) → corrected to anti-bakufu (both are Northern Alliance defectors per 工藤威 2002)
村上藩, 村松藩: originally coded anti-bakufu → corrected to pro-bakufu (both are Northern Alliance combatants per 工藤威 2002)
淀藩: originally coded neutral → corrected to anti-bakufu (castle-gate refusal at Toba-Fushimi 1868-01-28)

A 2026-05-29/30 vol. 2 re-extraction lifted troop coverage sharply; all four focal domains’ armies are now in Hansei Ichiran directly (incl. Saga, found in Table 9). Warships stay sparse by Hansei Ichiran’s construction; the navy of the focal domains comes from published supplements



The 96–100 percent coverage on every analytically critical column is the working summary of the data-quality work. The remaining gaps are documented and either structural (Hansei Ichiran’s military coverage was always sparse) or specific (the four missing kazoku rank rows are real historical exclusion events, not transcription failures).

22.3.1 2026-05-30 corrections (native-350-DPI re-reads)
A targeted re-read of three Hansei Ichiran entries at native resolution corrected three issues. The rebuilt dataset stays at 286 rows; a second rebuild produced zero further changes (the build is idempotent), and the four focal domains’ headline figures were unaffected.

佐賀 (Saga, focal) — the “military gap” was wrong. Saga does have a Volume 2 Table-9 military entry (2,972 troops / 36 cannon, book pp. 429–430) — it had been missed because Table 9 prints the modern 佐賀 while earlier searches used the old-kanji 佐嘉. The SOURCE_GAP rows were removed and the military-omitted / warships-absent flags cleared. Hansei Ichiran is now a citable primary for Saga’s army (the navy still comes from a published source). See the Saga walkthrough.
庄内 / 大泉 / 村岡 — a chimera + a duplicate. Hansei Ichiran records Shōnai under its 1869 name 大泉藩 (120,000 koku). A separate “庄内藩” row had been built from an entry mis-read as 鶴岡藩 — actually 邨岡藩 (村岡藩, Muraoka), an unrelated 11,000-koku tozama Yamana domain in Tajima whose HI numbers had been fused with Shōnai’s external identity. Result: Shōnai had been counted twice and Muraoka not at all. The fix de-duplicates Shōnai (single canonical 庄内藩, 譜代 / pro-bakufu / count) and restores 村岡藩 as its own row (外様 / pro-imperial / baron, 但馬/兵庫県).
岡山 (Okayama) — cross-page troop mis-attribution. Okayama’s recorded troops of 141 were the 銃士121 + 砲士20 tail of the previous domain (沼田藩 / Numata) that prints just right of the ○岡山藩 marker; Okayama’s real army (十七大隊 / 7,862 men) sits on the next page. Corrected to 7,862; the 141 tail re-tagged to Numata. A reminder that the cross-page one-domain shift can hit even large, focal-adjacent domains.

Full audit trail: CORRECTIONS_LOG.md (2026-05-30 entry); apply script apply_saga_muraoka_okayama_2026_05_30.py; backups *.bak_pre_saga_muraoka_okayama_2026_05_30.

title: 22  Data quality and the audit framework
text:
22.4 What the audit framework establishes
Three framings the dataset supports:

Replication-audited primary-source coverage: every analytically relevant variable has either a citable primary source or a citable scholarly-synthesis source; the values were cross-validated against Ravina (1999) at the ratio level; cross-page contamination was systematically audited and the four surfaced cases were corrected.
Transparent honest-medium tracking: the dataset maintains a per-cell tracker documenting every value that is not high-confidence, with the reason. Most empirical Meiji-history work either doesn’t disclose this or absorbs it silently; the present dataset surfaces it.
Documented cleanup trajectory: the cumulative reduction from 141 medium-confidence rows to 22 is recorded with per-row audit trail; a reviewer can trace any specific value’s provenance back to the source crop and to the cleanup decision that affected it.


Audit infrastructure summary:

Layer 1 (within-domain ratio sanity) — a ratio-based outlier detector; current state in sanity_check_audit.csv released alongside the dataset.
Layer 2 (cross-page contamination) — exact-match audit (stacked_page_contamination_audit.md) plus fuzzy near-match audit; documented contamination cases listed above.
Layer 3 (Ravina cross-validation) — ravina_replication_audit.csv per-domain delta against Ravina A1, with diagnoses for the 12 divergent cases.
Layer 4 (medium-confidence tracker) — honest_medium_tracker.csv with reason categories per row; cumulative trajectory in honest_medium_tracker_summary.md.

A single per-column provenance file (data_provenance.md) consolidates citation guidance, coverage rates, and known caveats for every variable.

Each polygon is a daimyō (大名) domain (藩) from 1870. You can toggle what the colours mean using the layer control in the top-right of the map.

text:
24.2 Sample scope
The 9 pages cover the four focal domains plus two pedagogically-important cases — Satsuma’s military-table 書ナラス non-disclosure and Saga’s recovered Table-9 army. Each page demonstrates a different HI structural pattern:

The kokudaka entry using Saga’s distinctive 貮口合 平均 (“two-mouth combined, average”) phrasing for what other domains call 合高. Saga has no vol1 entry — only vol2 — so this is its only kokudaka page.


8

Demographics (23,823 shizoku + 43,488 sotsu — sotsu is nearly double shizoku, an unusual ratio) followed by the start of ○佐倉藩 (Sakura-han, a different domain) on the same page — illustrating HI’s stacked-page pattern.


9
